studyMetadata.title: Girma cikin Almasihu (Kashi na 1)
studyMetadata.subtitle: Darasi na almajirantawa
studyMetadata.description: Shin kana girma cikin Almasihu? Allah na son duk ƴaƴansa su yi girma a cikin Yesu. Idan ba ka girma, wannan binciken zai taimaka wajen baiyana menene matsala rashin girman kuma ya za a yi ƙoƙari daidaita al'amuran. Idan kuma ka na girma, wannan binciken zai taimaka wurin ƙara wajen yin girma. Kowane sashe na buƙata amfini da Littafi Mai Tsarki tare da mai karatu ya bincikin kansa ta wurin yin tunani mai zurfi.

elements.text3: What is God really like? People have all sorts of ideas — some think of Him as distant and unknowable, others as a kind force, or a cosmic rule-giver. But we don’t have to guess. God has revealed Himself to us in the Bible, and that is where we begin.
Who is God?

elements.passageText1: <sup>3</sup>A kullum muna gode wa Allah, Uban Ubangijinmu Yesu Almasihu, duk sa'ad da muke yi muku addu'a,
elements.passageText2: <sup>3</sup>Kullum muna gode wa Allah, Uban Ubangijinmu Yesu Almasihu, a duk lokacin da muke adduˈa dominku.
elements.passageText5: <sup>3</sup>We always give thanks to God, the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, when we pray for you,

elements.question3: What does this verse tell us about who God is?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: This verse identifies God as ’the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ’, revealing that God is not an impersonal force but a personal being who stands in a Father-Son relationship with Jesus. It also shows that God is someone to whom we can give thanks and speak to in prayer.
elements.questionHint3: Paul uses a very specific title for God here — what does that title tell you about the kind of relationship God has with Jesus, and what might that imply about His nature?

elements.text3: What evidence is there that God exists?

elements.passageText1: <sup>1</sup>Dubi yadda sararin sama yake bayyana ɗaukakar Allah!
elements.passageText2: <sup>1</sup>Sammai suna shelar
elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>The heavens declare the glory of God; the sky displays his handiwork.

elements.question3: What does this verse suggest about the natural world as evidence for God?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The verse suggests that creation itself is a witness to God’s existence and glory. The sky and heavens are not neutral — they actively ’declare’ and ’display’ something about God. Just as a piece of artwork points to an artist, the created world points to a Creator. Evidence for God is not hidden away; it is visible above us every day.
elements.questionHint3: If the heavens are said to ’declare’ something, what does that imply about what an observer of the natural world should be able to conclude?

elements.passageText1: <sup>13</sup>Kai ne ka halicci kowace gaɓa ta jikina, <sup>14</sup>Ina yabonka gama kai abin tsoro ne,
elements.passageText2: <sup>13</sup>Kai ne ka halicci kayan cikina, <sup>14</sup>Ina yabonka,
elements.passageText5: <sup>13</sup>Certainly you made my mind and heart; you wove me together in my mother’s womb. <sup>14</sup>I will give you thanks because your deeds are awesome and amazing. You knew me thoroughly;

elements.question3: What does the psalmist’s description of being ’woven together’ suggest about God’s involvement in human life?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The image of being ’woven together’ suggests that human life is not an accident or a random biological event, but an intentional, careful act of craftsmanship by God. Just as a weaver works deliberately, thread by thread, God was personally involved in forming each person. This points to God’s intimate knowledge of and care for every individual human being from the very beginning of their existence.
elements.questionHint3: Think about what weaving involves — would you describe it as a careless, random process, or a deliberate and careful one? What does that tell you about how God formed you?

elements.passageText1: <sup>30</sup>Yesu ya yi waɗansu mu'ujizai da yawa dabam dabam a gaban almajiran, waɗanda ba a rubuta a littafin nan ba. <sup>31</sup>Amma an rubuta waɗannan ne, domin ku ba da gaskiya Yesu shi ne Almasihu, Ɗan Allah, ta wurin gaskatawa kuma ku sami rai a cikin sunansa.
elements.passageText2: <sup>30</sup>Yesu ya yi alamun ban mamaki da yawa a idanun almajiransa waɗanda ba a rubuta a wannan littafi ba. <sup>31</sup>Amma an rubuta waɗannan ne, domin ku ba da gaskiya cewa Yesu shi ne Almasihu, Ɗan Allah. Saˈan nan kuma, ta wurin ba da gaskiya ku sami rai a cikin sunansa.
elements.passageText5: <sup>30</sup>Now Jesus performed many other miraculous signs in the presence of the disciples, which are not recorded in this book. <sup>31</sup>But these are recorded so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name.

elements.question3: Why does John say he recorded these signs? What does this tell us about the purpose of the Bible?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: John says he recorded these signs so that readers would come to believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and through that belief find life in his name. This tells us that the Bible is not merely a historical record or a collection of moral teachings — it has an intentional, evangelistic purpose. It is written to bring people to faith and, through faith, to life.
elements.questionHint3: John tells us directly what outcome he was hoping for in his readers — can you spot the two goals he mentions, and how are they connected to each other?

elements.passageText1: <sup>1</sup>A zamanin dā, Allah ya yi wa kakannin kakanninmu magana ta hanyoyi masu yawa iri iri, ta bakin annabawa, <sup>2</sup>amma a zamanin nan na ƙarshe, sai ya yi mana magana ta wurin Ɗansa, wanda ya sa magājin kome, wanda ta wurinsa ne kuma ya halicci duniya. <sup>3</sup>Shi ne hasken ɗaukakar Allah, ainihin kamannin zatinsa kuma, shi kuma yana a riƙe da dukan abubuwa ta ikon faɗarsa. Bayan da ya tsarkake zunubanmu, sai ya zauna a dama da Maɗaukaki a can Sama.
elements.passageText2: <sup>1</sup>A zamanin dā, Allah ya yi wa kakanninmu magana ta hanyoyi iri-iri da kuma a lokatai dabam-dabam ta wurin annabawa. <sup>2</sup>Amma a waɗannan kwanakin ƙarshe ya yi mana magana ta wurin Ɗansa. Ta wurin Ɗan ne Allah ya halicci kome da kome. Shi ne kuma ya zama magājin kome. <sup>3</sup>Shi ne hasken ɗaukakar Allah da kuma ainihin kamannin Allah. Daidai yake kuma da Allah. Yana riƙe da dukan kome ta wurin kalmarsa mai iko. Bayan ya tsabtace mu daga zunubanmu, sai ya zauna a sama a hannun daman Mai Ɗaukaka duka.
elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>After God spoke long ago in various portions and in various ways to our ancestors through the prophets, <sup>2</sup>in these last days he has spoken to us in a son, whom he appointed heir of all things, and through whom he created the world. <sup>3</sup>The Son is the radiance of his glory and the representation of his essence, and he sustains all things by his powerful word, and so when he had accomplished cleansing for sins, he sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high.

elements.question3: How has God spoken to us, according to this passage? What does it tell us about Jesus’ relationship to God?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: According to this passage, God spoke in the past through the prophets in many different ways, but has now spoken definitively and finally through His Son, Jesus. The passage makes extraordinary claims about Jesus’ relationship to God: he is the ’radiance of his glory’ and the ’representation of his essence’, meaning that to see Jesus is to see what God is truly like. Jesus also created the world, sustains all things, and has now accomplished the cleansing of sins — pointing to a unique divine identity.
elements.questionHint3: The passage contrasts how God spoke ’long ago’ with how he has spoken ’in these last days’ — what is different about the two ways, and what does the description of the Son in verse 3 tell you about how closely he is linked to God himself?

elements.text3: What is God like?

elements.passageText1: <sup>3</sup>6.3 W.Yah 4.8 Suna ta kiran junansu suna cewa,
elements.passageText2: <sup>3</sup>Ɗaya yana tā da murya, wani kuma yana amsawa, suna cewa, 6.3 R. Yoh 4.8
elements.passageText5: And one called to another and said: 3’Holy, holy, holy is the Lord of Heaven’s Armies! His majestic splendor fills the entire earth!’

elements.question3: What does it mean for God to be described as ’holy’? Why do you think that word is repeated three times?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: For God to be ’holy’ means He is utterly set apart — morally perfect, pure, and completely unlike anything in the created world. Holiness is not just one quality among many but speaks to His absolute otherness and majesty. The threefold repetition (‘holy, holy, holy’) is the strongest form of emphasis in Hebrew — it is not just saying God is very holy, but that holiness defines His very being to the highest possible degree. No other attribute of God is praised in this tripled way in Scripture.
elements.questionHint3: In everyday language, if you want to stress how extremely something is true, what do you do — and how might that same idea apply to why the angels repeat the word ’holy’ three times rather than just once?

elements.passageText1: <sup>7</sup>Da kun san ni, da kun san Ubana ma. Amma daga yanzu kun san shi, kun kuma gan shi.” <sup>8</sup>Sai Filibus ya ce masa, “Ya Ubangiji, ka nuna mana Uban, bukatarmu ta biya ke nan.” <sup>9</sup>Yesu ya ce masa, “Na daɗe tare da ku haka, amma har yanzu ba ka san ni ba, Filibus? elements.passageText2: <sup>7</sup>Da kun san ni, da kun san Ubana ma. Amma daga yanzu kun san shi, kun kuma gan shi.” <sup>8</sup>Sai Filibus ya ce masa, “Ya Ubangiji, in ka nuna mana Uban, to, ai, bukatarmu ta biya ke nan.” <sup>9</sup>Yesu ya ce masa, “Na daɗe tare da ku haka, amma har yanzu ba ka san ni ba, Filibus? Duk wanda ya gan ni, ai, ya ga Uban. Yaya za ka ce, ‘Nuna mana Uban’?
elements.passageText5: <sup>7</sup>If you have known me, you will know my Father too. And from now on you do know him and have seen him.’ <sup>8</sup>Philip said to him, ‘Lord, show us the Father, and we will be content.’ <sup>9</sup>Jesus replied, ‘Have I been with you for so long, and you have not known me, Philip? The person who has seen me has seen the Father! How can you say, "Show us the Father"?’

elements.question3: What does Jesus claim about the relationship between Himself and the Father? What does that mean for how we understand God?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus makes a remarkable claim: that to have seen Him is to have seen the Father. He is not merely a messenger from God, or someone who teaches about God — He is the very image and presence of God. This means that if we want to know what God is like, we do not need to look further than Jesus: His compassion, His honesty, His treatment of people, His willingness to lay down His life — all of these reveal the character of God the Father.
elements.questionHint3: Philip asks to be shown the Father as if the Father were somewhere else — why does Jesus find this question so surprising, and what does His answer imply about where the Father can actually be found?

elements.passageText1: <sup>2</sup>Kafin a kafa tuddai, ... <sup>4</sup>90.4 2Bit 3.8 A gare ka shekara dubu, kamar kwana ɗaya ne,
elements.passageText2: <sup>2</sup>Tun kafin haifuwar manyan tuddai, ... <sup>4</sup>Gama a idanunka shekara dubu 90.4 2 Bit 3.8
elements.passageText5: <sup>2</sup>Even before the mountains were born, or you gave birth to the earth and the world, from eternity to eternity you are God. <sup>4</sup>Yes, in your eyes a thousand years are like yesterday that quickly passes, or like one of the divisions of the nighttime.

elements.question3: What does this passage tell us about God’s relationship to time?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: This passage tells us that God exists outside of and before time itself — He was God before the earth or mountains existed, and He will continue to be God forever. Verse 4 shows that God does not experience time as we do: what feels like a thousand years to us is like a single day to Him. God is not constrained by time the way we are; He sees all of history as present to Him at once, which means He is never caught off guard, never in a hurry, and never late.
elements.questionHint3: The psalmist says God existed even before the mountains or the earth were ’born’ — what does that tell you about whether God had a beginning, and how might that affect the way He experiences time compared to us?

elements.passageText1: <sup>17</sup>Kowace kyakkyawar baiwa, da kowace cikakkiyar kyauta, daga Sama suke, sun sauko ne daga wurin Uban haskoki, wanda ba sauyawa ko wata alamar sākewa a gare shi faufau.
elements.passageText2: <sup>17</sup>Kowace baiwa mai kyau, da kowace cikakkiyar kyauta daga sama suke. Sun sauko ne daga wurin Allah Uba wanda ya halicci rana, da wata, da taurari duka 1.17 Allah Uba wanda ya halicci rana, da wata, da taurari duka: Da Helenanci an rubuta “Uban Haskoki” ne a nan. . Shi kuwa ba ya canjawa, ba ya juye-juye kamar inuwa.
elements.passageText5: <sup>17</sup>All generous giving and every perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of lights, with whom there is no variation or the slightest hint of change.

elements.question3: What does it mean that there is ’no variation or the slightest hint of change’ in God? Why is that significant?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: It means that God’s character, His love, His promises, and His goodness never shift or fluctuate. Unlike human relationships — where people can be kind one day and cold the next — God is completely consistent. This is enormously significant because it means we can trust Him fully. His love for us today is exactly as strong as it was yesterday and will be tomorrow. His promises do not expire or get revised. We never have to worry that God has changed His mind about us.
elements.questionHint3: Think about a time when someone’s changing moods or broken promises made it hard to trust them — how might God being completely unchanging make a difference to how you relate to Him?

elements.passageText1: <sup>7</sup>Ina zan tafi in tsere wa Ruhunka? <sup>8</sup>Idan na hau cikin samaniya kana can, <sup>9</sup>In na tashi sama, na tafi, na wuce gabas, <sup>10</sup>Kana can domin ka bi da ni,
elements.passageText2: <sup>7</sup>Ina zan je domin in kauce wa ruhunka? <sup>8</sup>Idan na hau sammai, kana can, <sup>9</sup>In na tashi kamar tsuntsu <sup>10</sup>ko a can ɗin ma hannunka zai bi da ni,
elements.passageText5: <sup>7</sup>Where can I go to escape your Spirit? Where can I flee to escape your presence? <sup>8</sup>If I were to ascend to heaven, you would be there. If I were to sprawl out in Sheol, there you would be. <sup>9</sup>If I were to fly away on the wings of the dawn, and settle down on the other side of the ocean, <sup>10</sup>even there your hand would guide me, your right hand would grab hold of me.

elements.question3: What does this passage tell us about God’s presence? Is that a comforting or a challenging thought for you?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: This passage tells us that God is omnipresent — there is literally nowhere in the universe, from the heights of heaven to the depths of Sheol, where God is absent. His presence is inescapable. Whether that is comforting or challenging likely depends on how we feel about God. For someone who trusts God and longs for His help, it is deeply comforting — no matter how far or alone we feel, His hand is still guiding us. But it can also feel challenging or exposing, because it means nothing in our lives is hidden from Him.
elements.questionHint3: The psalmist lists extreme opposites — heaven and Sheol, near and far — to make a point about God’s presence. What conclusion is he building towards, and does knowing God is always present feel like good news or an uncomfortable truth to you personally?

elements.passageText1: <sup>8</sup>Wanda ba ya ƙauna, bai san Allah ba sam, domin Allah shi ne ƙauna.
elements.passageText2: <sup>8</sup>Duk wanda ba ya nuna ƙauna, bai san Allah ba sam, gama Allah ƙauna ne.
elements.passageText5: <sup>8</sup>The person who does not love does not know God, because God is love.

elements.question3: John says ’God is love’ — not just that God loves, but that love defines who He is. What difference does that make?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Saying God loves is saying something He does — an action He can choose or withhold. But saying God is love means love is inseparable from His very being; He cannot stop being loving any more than He can stop being God. It means His love is not a response to us being lovable — it flows from who He is. This also means that love is not just a feeling God has but the core of His identity, which gives us grounds to trust that His love is permanent, unconditional, and inexhaustible.
elements.questionHint3: Consider the difference between saying ’a teacher is kind’ and ’a teacher sometimes acts kindly’ — which says more about their core identity, and how does that distinction apply to what John is claiming about God?

elements.text3: How do we know that God loves us?

elements.passageText1: <sup>8</sup>Amma kuwa Allah yana tabbatar mana da ƙaunar da yake yi mana, wato tun muna masu zunubi, Almasihu ya mutu dominmu.
elements.passageText2: <sup>8</sup>Amma Allah ya tabbatar mana yawan ƙaunarsa, da yake tun muna masu zunubi, Almasihu ya mutu saboda mu.
elements.passageText5: <sup>8</sup>But God demonstrates his own love for us, in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.

elements.question3: How does Paul say God has demonstrated His love? What is striking about the timing — ‘while we were still sinners’?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul says God demonstrated His love through the death of Christ on our behalf. What is especially striking is the timing: God did not wait for us to clean up our lives, earn His favour, or become worthy of His love. Christ died for us while we were still sinners — still in rebellion against God. This overturns the human assumption that love must be deserved. God’s love is not a reward for good behaviour; it is a free, initiating love given to those who had done nothing to merit it.
elements.questionHint3: In most human relationships, we tend to show kindness to people who have been kind to us — so what is surprising or unexpected about the specific moment Paul says God chose to demonstrate His love?

elements.passageText1: <sup>9</sup>Ta haka aka bayyana ƙaunar Allah gare mu, da Allah ya aiko da Ɗansa makaɗaici a duniya, domin mu sami rai madawwami ta wurinsa. <sup>10</sup>Ta haka ƙauna take, wato, ba mu ne muka ƙaunaci Allah ba, sai dai shi ne ya ƙaunace mu, ya aiko Ɗansa, hadayar sulhu saboda a gafarta zunubanmu.
elements.passageText2: <sup>9</sup>Ga yadda Allah ya nuna mana ƙaunarsa. Ya aiko da makaɗaicin Ɗansa zuwa cikin duniya domin mu sami rai ta wurinsa. <sup>10</sup>Ga yadda ƙauna ta gaskiya take, wato ba mu ne muka ƙaunaci Allah ba, sai dai shi ne ya ƙaunace mu, har ya aiko da Ɗansa domin yă zama hadaya ta ɗaukar alhakin zunubanmu.
elements.passageText5: <sup>9</sup>By this the love of God is revealed in us: that God has sent his one and only Son into the world so that we may live through him. <sup>10</sup>In this is love: not that we have loved God, but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the atoning sacrifice for our sins.

elements.question3: According to John, who took the initiative in this relationship — God or us? What does that tell us about the nature of God’s love?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: John is explicit that God took the initiative — love is defined here not as our love for God, but as His love for us. God moved first. He sent His Son before we sought Him, to deal with the sin that separated us from Him. This tells us that God’s love is not reactive or conditional — it is originating and unconditional. We do not draw God’s love out of Him by our devotion; He loved us freely and acted on that love at great cost, sending His only Son as a sacrifice.
elements.questionHint3: John says ’not that we have loved God, but that he loved us’ — who does he say made the first move, and what practical difference might it make to you personally to know that the relationship started with God, not with you?

elements.text3: Key truths

It is foolish to think that there is no God.

"The fool says in his heart, ‘There is no God.’" — Psalm 14:1a

God loves us and longs that we might respond to His love.

"See what great love the Father has lavished on us, that we should be called children of God! And that is what we are!" — 1 John 3:1a
To think about

elements.question3: How would you try to convince a friend that God does actually exist?
If asked the question, ‘What is God like?’, what would be the main characteristics you would mention?

chapters.chapterTitle3: Jesus Christ, the Son of God

elements.text3: Many people think of Jesus as a great teacher or a good man who lived two thousand years ago. But the Bible presents Someone far greater. Surprisingly, Jesus did not begin at Bethlehem — He has always existed. In this study we look at who Jesus really is.
What do we know about Jesus before He came to earth?

elements.passageText1: <sup>1</sup>Tun fil'azal akwai Kalma, Kalman nan kuwa tare da Allah yake, Kalman nan kuwa Allah ne. <sup>2</sup>Shi ne tun fil'azal yake tare da Allah. <sup>3</sup>Dukan abubuwa sun kasance ta gare shi ne, ba kuma abin da ya kasance na abubuwan da suka kasance, sai ta game da shi.
elements.passageText2: <sup>1</sup>Tun farko akwai Kalma, Kalman kuwa tare da Allah yake, Kalman kuwa Allah ne. <sup>2</sup>Shi ne wanda tun farko yake tare da Allah. <sup>3</sup>Ta wurinsa ne aka yi dukan abubuwa, kuma babu wani abu a cikin dukan halittar da aka yi wanda ba tare da shi aka yi ba.
elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was fully God. <sup>2</sup>The Word was with God in the beginning. <sup>3</sup>All things were created by him, and apart from him not one thing was created that has been created.
elements.note: In this passage the ’Word’ is referring to Jesus.

elements.question3: John calls Jesus ’the Word’. What does this passage tell us about where Jesus was before He came to earth?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Before coming to earth, Jesus — the Word — already existed. He was with God in the beginning, meaning He has always been in close relationship with the Father. More than that, He was fully God Himself. John also tells us that all things were created through Him, meaning Jesus was not a created being who appeared at some point in history, but the eternal Creator who was present before anything else existed.
elements.questionHint3: John says the Word was ’in the beginning’ — does that suggest Jesus had a starting point, or that He was already there before anything began? What else does John say about His role before creation?

elements.passageText1: <sup>15</sup>Shi ne surar Allah marar ganuwa, magāji ne tun ba a halicci kome ba, <sup>16</sup>don ta gare shi ne aka halicci dukkan kome, abubuwan da suke a sama da abubuwan da suke a ƙasa, masu ganuwa da marasa ganuwa, ko kursiyai, ko masu mulki, ko masarauta, ko masu iko, wato, dukkan abubuwa an halicce su ne ta wurinsa, shi ne kuma makomarsu. <sup>17</sup>Shi ne a gaba da dukkan abubuwa, dukkan abubuwa kuma shi ne yake riƙe da su.
elements.passageText2: <sup>15</sup>Almasihu shi ne kamannin Allah wanda ba a iya gani, shi ne Ɗan fari gaban dukan halitta. <sup>16</sup>Gama ta wurinsa ne aka yi kome da kome, abubuwan da suke a sammai da kuma a nan duniya, da abubuwan da ake gani da waɗanda ba a iya gani, ko kujerun mulki, ko mulkoki, ko masu sarauta, ko ikoki. Dukan abubuwan da aka halitta, an halicce su ne ta wurinsa da kuma dominsa. <sup>17</sup>Almasihu shi ne a gaba da dukan kome, ta wurinsa ne kuma dukan halitta ta kasance a harhaɗe.
elements.passageText5: <sup>15</sup>He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation. <sup>16</sup>For by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible, whether thrones or dominions or rulers or authorities—all things were created through him and for him. <sup>17</sup>And he is before all things, and in him all things hold together.

elements.question3: What does Paul say Jesus’ role was in creation? What does verse 17 suggest about His ongoing relationship to the universe?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul says that all things — in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible — were created by Jesus, through Jesus, and for Jesus. His role in creation was total: nothing came into existence apart from Him. Verse 17 then goes further, saying that in Him all things ’hold together’. This means Jesus is not just the originator of creation but its ongoing sustainer — the universe continues to exist and cohere because of Him. He is not a distant figure who started things and stepped away; He is actively involved in holding everything together at this very moment.
elements.questionHint3: Paul uses three prepositions in verse 16 — all things were created ’by’, ‘through’, and ’for’ Jesus. What does adding those three together suggest about the scope of His involvement in creation, and how does verse 17 extend that idea into the present?

elements.text3: Why was He called Jesus?

elements.passageText2: <sup>21</sup>Za ta haifi ɗa, za ka sa masa suna Yesu 1.21 Yesu: Sunan Yesu a Helenanci, shi ne Yoshuwa a Ibraniyanci. Maˈanar sunan kuwa ita ce Allah yana ceto. domin shi ne zai ceci mutanensa daga zunubansu.” 1.21 Luk 1.31
elements.passageText5: <sup>21</sup>She will give birth to a son and you will name him Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins.

elements.question3: What does the name ’Jesus’ mean, and what does that tell us about the purpose of His coming?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The angel explains the name directly: Jesus means ’he will save his people from their sins’. The name itself is a mission statement. Jesus did not come primarily as a political liberator, a moral teacher, or a miracle worker — He came to deal with the problem of sin. His name tells us that rescue from sin is the central purpose of His life, and that this rescue was planned and announced before He was even born.
elements.questionHint3: The angel gives a reason for the name with the word ’for’ — what specific problem does He say Jesus will come to solve, and does that surprise you given what people might have expected a Saviour to do?

elements.text3: How do we know that Jesus is truly human?

elements.passageText1: <sup>7</sup>Sai ta haifi ɗanta na fari, ta rufi shi da zanen goyo, ta kuma kwantar da shi a wani komi dabbobi, don ba su sami ɗaki a masaukin ba. ... <sup>40</sup>Ɗan yaron kuwa ya girma, ya kawo ƙarfi, yana mai matuƙar hikima. Alherin Allah kuwa yana tare da shi.
elements.passageText2: <sup>7</sup>Ta haifi ɗanta na fari, ta rufe shi da zanen goyo, ta kuma kwantar da shi a abin da ake ba dabbobi abinci a ciki, domin ba su sami ɗaki a masauki ba. ... <sup>40</sup>Yaron ya yi girma, ya kuma yi ƙarfi, yana cike da hikima. Alherin Allah kuma yana tare da shi.
elements.passageText5: <sup>7</sup>And she gave birth to her firstborn son and wrapped him in strips of cloth and laid him in a manger, because there was no place for them in the inn. ... <sup>40</sup>The child grew and became strong, filled with wisdom, and the favor of God was upon him.

elements.question3: What details in these verses show that Jesus experienced a genuinely human childhood?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Verse 7 shows that Jesus was born in the ordinary, physical way — wrapped in cloth like any newborn infant and laid in a manger. He entered the world in vulnerability and poverty, not in heavenly splendour. Verse 40 then tells us He grew and became strong, and was filled with wisdom over time. This is the pattern of a normal human childhood: development, growth, and gradual maturation. Jesus was not born fully formed or all-knowing in His human experience — He grew up just as we do.
elements.questionHint3: Verse 40 says Jesus ’grew’ and ’became strong’ — do those words suggest something that happened all at once or over time, and what does that tell you about whether His childhood was similar to ours?

elements.passageText1: <sup>2</sup>har kwana arba'in, Iblis yana gwada shi. A kwanakin nan bai ci kome ba. Da suka ƙare kuwa ya ji yunwa.
elements.passageText2: <sup>2</sup>inda Shaiɗan ya gwada shi har kwana arbaˈin. A ƙarshen wannan lokaci, sai yunwa ta kama shi, don bai ci kome ba.
elements.passageText5: Jesus was led by the Spirit in the wilderness, 2where for forty days he endured temptations from the devil. He ate nothing during those days, and when they were completed, he was famished.

elements.question3: What does it mean that Jesus ’was famished’? How does this confirm His humanity?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: That Jesus ’was famished’ means He experienced real, intense physical hunger — the kind that comes from forty days without food. This is not a metaphor; it is a physiological reality. A purely divine being would not experience bodily hunger, but Jesus did. This confirms that His human body was genuine and subject to the same physical limitations as ours: He needed food, He felt the pain of going without it, and His body was weakened by the experience. His humanity was not a costume — it was real.
elements.questionHint3: Think about what hunger feels like after missing just one meal — what does experiencing it after forty days tell you about whether Jesus had a real human body with real physical needs?

elements.passageText1: <sup>23</sup>Da ya shiga jirgi, almajiransa suka bi shi. <sup>24</sup>Sai ga wani babban hadiri ya taso a teku, har raƙuman ruwa suka fara shan kan jirgin, amma yana barci.
elements.passageText2: <sup>23</sup>Da ya shiga jirgin ruwa, sai almajiransa suka bi shi. <sup>24</sup>Sai babban hadari mai iska ya taso a tekun har ruwan yana tashi yana shiga cikin jirgin, amma Yesu yana nan yana barci.
elements.passageText5: <sup>23</sup>As he got into the boat, his disciples followed him. <sup>24</sup>And suddenly a great storm developed on the sea, so that the waves began to swamp the boat; but he was asleep.

elements.question3: What is humanly significant about the detail that Jesus was asleep during the storm?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Sleep is one of the most fundamental marks of being human — it reflects physical tiredness and the body’s need for rest. The fact that Jesus was asleep in the boat, even in the middle of a violent storm, tells us He had been working hard enough to be genuinely exhausted. He was not pretending to be tired; He actually needed rest. This small detail quietly but powerfully confirms that His humanity was authentic: He got tired just as we do.
elements.questionHint3: Why do human beings need to sleep at all, and what does needing sleep tell you about the kind of body a person has — could someone who never truly got tired fall into a deep sleep during a dangerous storm?

elements.passageText1: <sup>32</sup>Sai soja suka zo suka karya ƙafafun na farkon nan, da kuma na ɗayan da aka gicciye tare da shi. <sup>33</sup>Amma da suka zo wurin Yesu, suka kuma ga ya riga ya mutu, ba su karya ƙafafunsa ba.
elements.passageText2: <sup>32</sup>Sai sojojin suka zo wurin mutanen da aka gicciye tare da Yesu suka kakkarya ƙafafun mutum na farko, saˈan nan suka je suka kakkarya ƙafafun mutum na biyun. <sup>33</sup>Amma da suka zo wurin Yesu, ba su kakkarya ƙafafunsa ba domin sun ga ya riga ya mutu.
elements.passageText5: <sup>32</sup>So the soldiers came and broke the legs of the two men who had been crucified with Jesus, first the one and then the other. <sup>33</sup>But when they came to Jesus and saw that he was already dead, they did not break his legs.

elements.question3: Why is it significant that Jesus actually died on the cross? What does John want us to understand here?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: John records that the soldiers — professionals whose job was to ensure death — confirmed that Jesus was already dead. They had no reason to spare Him and every reason to make sure. The fact that He was genuinely dead is significant on two levels. First, it confirms His full humanity: a divine being who merely appeared human could not truly die. Second, it establishes that the resurrection was a real resurrection from actual death, not a recovery from unconsciousness. John is providing eyewitness evidence that the death was real, so that the reader can trust that the resurrection was equally real.
elements.questionHint3: The soldiers broke the legs of the others to speed their deaths — so why is it significant that they did not need to do this to Jesus, and what were they effectively confirming by leaving Him alone?

elements.text3: How do we know that Jesus is truly God?

elements.passageText1: <sup>1</sup>Tun fil'azal akwai Kalma, Kalman nan kuwa tare da Allah yake, Kalman nan kuwa Allah ne.
elements.passageText2: <sup>1</sup>Tun farko akwai Kalma, Kalman kuwa tare da Allah yake, Kalman kuwa Allah ne.
elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was fully God.

elements.question3: John says the Word ’was fully God’. How does this verse set the foundation for understanding who Jesus is?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: John opens his Gospel with a deliberate echo of Genesis — ‘In the beginning’ — and immediately places Jesus (the Word) there, before anything else existed. He then makes two statements in one breath: the Word was with God (pointing to a distinct, personal relationship) and the Word was fully God (pointing to a shared divine nature). This verse establishes from the outset that Jesus cannot be understood as merely a great man or even a lesser divine being — He is fully God, and the rest of John’s Gospel is written to demonstrate what that means.
elements.questionHint3: John says the Word was both ’with God’ and ’was fully God’ — do those two statements contradict each other, or could they together be pointing to something about Jesus that is more complex than a simple either/or?

elements.passageText1: <sup>5</sup>Da Yesu ya ga bangaskiyarsu sai ya ce wa shanyayyen, “Ɗana, an gafarta maka zunubanka.” <sup>6</sup>To, waɗansu malaman Attaura na nan zaune, suna ta wuswasi a zuciya tasu, <sup>7</sup>suna cewa, “Don me mutumin nan yake faɗar haka? Ai, sāɓo yake! Wa yake da ikon gafarta zunubi banda Allah kaɗai?” <sup>8</sup>Nan da nan, da Yesu ya gane a ransa suna ta wuswasi haka a zuci, sai ya ce musu, “Don me kuke wuswasi haka a zuciyarku? <sup>9</sup>Wanne ya fi sauƙi, a ce wa shanyayyen, ‘An gafarta maka zunubanka’, ko kuwa a ce, ‘Tashi ka ɗauki shimfiɗarka ka yi tafiya’? <sup>10</sup>Amma don ku sakankance Ɗan Mutum na da ikon gafarta zunubi a duniya” sai ya ce wa shanyayyen, <sup>11</sup>“Na ce maka, tashi ka ɗauki shimfiɗarka ka tafi gida.” <sup>12</sup>Sai ya tashi nan da nan, ya ɗauki shimfiɗarsa, ya fita a gaban idon kowa, har suka yi mamaki duka, suka ɗaukaka Allah suna cewa, “Ba mu taɓa ganin irin haka ba!”
elements.passageText2: <sup>5</sup>Da Yesu ya ga bangaskiyarsu sai ya ce wa shanyayyen, “Ɗana, an gafarta maka zunubanka.” <sup>6</sup>To, waɗansu malaman Koyarwar Musa suna zaune a wurin, suna ta tunani a cikin zuciyarsu, <sup>7</sup>suna cewa, “Don me mutumin nan yake magana haka? Ai, yana wa Allah saɓo ne! Wa yake da ikon gafarta zunubi in ba Allah kaɗai ba?” <sup>8</sup>Nan da nan Yesu ya gane abin da suke tunani a zuciyarsu, sai ya ce musu, “Me ya sa kuke yin irin waɗannan tunani? <sup>9</sup>Wanne ya fi sauƙi, a ce wa mai ciwon nan, ‘An gafarta maka zunubanka,’ ko kuwa a ce, ‘Tashi ka ɗauki tabarmarka ka yi tafiya?’ <sup>10</sup>Amma ina so ku sani cewa Ɗan Mutum yana da iko yă gafarta zunubai a duniya.” Sai ya ce wa shanyayyen, <sup>11</sup>“Na ce maka, tashi, ka ɗauki tabarmarka ka tafi gida.” <sup>12</sup>Nan da nan ya tashi, ya ɗauki tabarmarsa, ya fita. Dukan mutane kuma suna kallonsa, suna mamaki, suna ɗaukaka Allah suna cewa, “Ba mu taɓa ganin wani abu kamar haka ba.”
elements.passageText5: <sup>5</sup>When Jesus saw their faith, he said to the paralytic, ‘Child, your sins are forgiven.’ <sup>6</sup>Now some of the experts in the law were sitting there, questioning in their hearts, <sup>7</sup>’Why does this man speak this way? He is blaspheming! Who can forgive sins but God alone?’ <sup>8</sup>Now immediately, when Jesus realized in his spirit that they were contemplating such thoughts, he said to them, ‘Why are you thinking such things in your hearts? <sup>9</sup>Which is easier, to say to the paralytic, "Your sins are forgiven," or to say, "Stand up, take your mat, and walk"? <sup>10</sup>But so that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins’—he said to the paralytic— <sup>11</sup>’I tell you, stand up, take your mat, and go home.’ <sup>12</sup>And he stood up, took his mat immediately, and went out before them all. They were all amazed and glorified God, saying, ‘We’ve never seen anything like this!’

elements.question3: The religious leaders ask, ‘Who can forgive sins but God alone?’ How does Jesus’ response answer their question?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The religious leaders correctly identify that only God can forgive sins — and Jesus does not correct them on that point. Instead, He accepts the premise and then acts on it. He heals the paralysed man as visible proof that He has the authority to do what only God can do: forgive sins. Jesus is not claiming to forgive sins on God’s behalf, as a priest might; He is claiming to do it in His own right. His question — ‘which is easier?’ — is a trap: since both are impossible for a mere human, doing one publicly proves the authority to do the other. The miracle is His answer to their question.
elements.questionHint3: The religious leaders’ logic was: only God forgives sins, therefore Jesus is blaspheming by claiming to do it — but what does Jesus do next, and how does that action respond to the charge rather than simply arguing against it?

elements.passageText1: <sup>8</sup>Sai Filibus ya ce masa, “Ya Ubangiji, ka nuna mana Uban, bukatarmu ta biya ke nan.” <sup>9</sup>Yesu ya ce masa, “Na daɗe tare da ku haka, amma har yanzu ba ka san ni ba, Filibus? Duk wanda ya gan ni, ai, ya ga Uban. Yaya za ka ce, ‘Nuna mana Uban’”?
elements.passageText2: <sup>8</sup>Sai Filibus ya ce masa, “Ya Ubangiji, in ka nuna mana Uban, to, ai, bukatarmu ta biya ke nan.” <sup>9</sup>Yesu ya ce masa, “Na daɗe tare da ku haka, amma har yanzu ba ka san ni ba, Filibus? Duk wanda ya gan ni, ai, ya ga Uban. Yaya za ka ce, ‘Nuna mana Uban’?
elements.passageText5: <sup>8</sup>Philip said to him, ‘Lord, show us the Father, and we will be content.’ <sup>9</sup>Jesus replied, ‘Have I been with you for so long, and you have not known me, Philip? The person who has seen me has seen the Father! How can you say, "Show us the Father"?’

elements.question3: What claim is Jesus making when He says ’whoever has seen me has seen the Father’?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus is claiming that He is not merely a representative or messenger of the Father, but that He so perfectly reveals and embodies the Father that seeing Him is seeing the Father. This is an extraordinary claim: it means that if you want to know what God is like — His compassion, His honesty, His attitude to sinners, His willingness to suffer — you look at Jesus. Jesus does not point people away from Himself to find God; He says that God is found in Him. It is a direct claim to share in the divine nature and identity of the Father.
elements.questionHint3: Philip wanted to be shown the Father as if Jesus and the Father were two entirely separate things to find — why does Jesus find this question so bewildering, and what does His surprise tell you about how closely He sees Himself as connected to the Father?

Sai suka tashe shi, suka ce masa, “Malam, za mu hallaka ba ka kula ba ko?” <sup>39</sup>Sai ya farka, ya tsawata wa iskar, ya kuma ce wa ruwan tekun, “Natsu! Ka yi shiru!” Sai iskar ta kwanta, wurin duk ya yi tsit. <sup>40</sup>Ya ce musu, “Don me kuka firgita haka? Har yanzu ba ku da bangaskiya ne?” <sup>41</sup>Sai suka tsorata, matuƙar tsoro, suka ce wa juna, “Wa ke nan kuma, wanda har iska da ruwan teku ma suke yi masa biyayya?”

Ruwa kuwa yana shiga cikin jirgin har jirgin ya kusan cika da ruwa. <sup>38</sup>Yesu kuwa yana can bayan jirgin yana barci kansa a matashi. Ka yi shiru!” Sai iskar ta daina, wuri duk ya yi tsit. <sup>40</sup>Ya ce wa almajiransa, “Me ya sa kuka ji tsoro haka? elements.passageText5: <sup>37</sup>Now a great windstorm developed and the waves were breaking into the boat, so that the boat was nearly swamped. <sup>38</sup>But he was in the stern, sleeping on a cushion. They woke him up and said to him, ‘Teacher, don’t you care that we are about to die?’ <sup>39</sup>So he got up and rebuked the wind, and said to the sea, ‘Be quiet! Calm down!’ Then the wind stopped, and it was dead calm. <sup>40</sup>And he said to them, ‘Why are you cowardly? Do you still not have faith?’ <sup>41</sup>They were overwhelmed with fear and said to one another, ‘Who then is this? Even the wind and the sea obey him!’

elements.question3: The disciples ask ’Who then is this?’ after Jesus calms the storm. What answer does this miracle point to?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The disciples’ question is the right one, and the miracle itself supplies the answer. In the Old Testament, it is God who commands the seas and the winds — nature obeys its Creator. When Jesus rebukes the storm and it immediately calms, He is doing something that only God does. The disciples were not just impressed; they were ’overwhelmed with fear’ — a response that in Scripture is typically prompted by an encounter with the divine. The miracle points to the answer: this is God Himself in human form, the one who made the sea and therefore has authority over it.
elements.questionHint3: In the Old Testament, who is described as having authority over the sea and storms? Knowing that, what conclusion does it seem like Mark wants the reader to draw from the disciples’ question?

Yesu ya ce musu, “Ku kwance masa, ya tafi.”

Wanda yake ba da gaskiya gare ni, ko ya mutu zai rayu. ... <sup>43</sup>Da ya faɗa haka sai ya ɗaga murya da ƙarfi ya ce, “Liˈazaru, fito!” <sup>44</sup>Sai mutumin da ya mutu ɗin ya fito, ƙafa da hannu a ɗaure da guntayen yadi, fuskarsa kuma a naɗe take da zanen da aka binne shi da shi.
elements.passageText5: <sup>25</sup>Jesus said to her, ‘I am the resurrection and the life. The one who believes in me will live even if he dies.’ ... <sup>43</sup>When he had said this, he shouted in a loud voice, ‘Lazarus, come out!’ <sup>44</sup>The one who had died came out, his feet and hands tied up with strips of cloth, and a cloth wrapped around his face. Jesus said to them, ‘Unwrap him and let him go.’

elements.question3: Jesus says ’I am the resurrection and the life’ — then demonstrates it. What does this tell us about His authority over death?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus does not say He can bring resurrection or that He knows the way to life — He says He is the resurrection and the life. It is an identity claim, not just a capability claim. He then immediately backs it up by calling a man who had been dead for four days out of his tomb with a single command. Death had no power to resist Him. This tells us that Jesus’ authority over death is not borrowed or limited — it is intrinsic to who He is. And because He is the resurrection and the life, the promise He makes to believers — that they will live even if they die — rests on His own identity and power.
elements.questionHint3: Notice that Jesus says ’I am the resurrection’ rather than ’I can perform resurrections’ — what is the difference between those two statements, and what does it suggest about where the power over death actually resides?

elements.question: What do you think of when you hear Jesus described as ’the Son of God’? Think about it before tapping ’Find out’.
elements.answer: People sometimes assume this means Jesus is in some way less than God — a divine being, perhaps, but not fully God Himself. The Bible, however, uses ’Son of God’ to mean the opposite. When Jesus claimed to be the Son of God, his listeners understood it as a claim to be equal with God (John 5:18). ’Son of’ in this context conveys nature and identity, not origin or rank. Jesus is fully God — not a lesser divine being, and not a created figure who became divine.

elements.text3: Was Jesus tempted? Did He sin?

elements.passageText1: <sup>15</sup>Gama Babban Firist da muke da shi ba marar juyayin kasawarmu ba ne, a'a, shi ne wanda aka jarabce shi ta kowace hanya da aka jarabce mu, amma bai yi zunubi ba.
elements.passageText2: <sup>15</sup>Gama Babban Firist nan namu ba wani ne wanda bai damu da kāsawarmu ba. Aˈa, shi ne wanda aka gwada shi ta kowace hanyar da akan gwada mu, amma fa bai yi wani zunubi ba.
elements.passageText5: <sup>15</sup>For we do not have a high priest incapable of sympathizing with our weaknesses, but one who has been tempted in every way just as we are, yet without sin.

elements.question3: According to this verse, how does Jesus’ experience of temptation affect His ability to help us when we are tempted?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The writer says that because Jesus was tempted in every way just as we are, He is able to sympathise with our weaknesses — not from a distance, but from personal experience. He is not a high priest who is immune to the pressures we face; He knows what it is to feel the pull of temptation from the inside. This means that when we come to Him in our struggle, we are not coming to someone who shrugs and says ’I don’t understand what that feels like.’ We are coming to one who does understand, and who — crucially — came through it without sin, which means He knows the way through.
elements.questionHint3: Think about whether you would rather seek help from someone who has only read about a difficult experience or from someone who has actually been through it themselves — how does that apply to what this verse is saying about Jesus?

elements.text3: Key truths

Jesus is God (the Son of God) and He has always existed.

"In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God." — John 1:1

Because Jesus lived as a human being, He truly understands the temptations and problems we face.

"For we do not have a high priest who is unable to feel sympathy for our weaknesses, but we have one who has been tempted in every way, just as we are – yet he did not sin." — Hebrews 4:15
To think about

elements.question3: How would you try to convince your friends that Jesus was more than a good teacher?
If asked the question, ‘What is Jesus like?’, how would you answer?

elements.text3: The Holy Spirit is perhaps the least understood member of the Trinity. Many people think of Him as an impersonal force or a vague spiritual feeling. But the Bible is clear: He is a person — the third person of the Trinity, alongside God the Father and God the Son. The Holy Spirit is also referred to in the Bible as the Spirit of Jesus (and of Christ and of God’s Son).
How do we know that the Holy Spirit is a person?

elements.passageText1: <sup>26</sup>Haka kuma Ruhu yake yi mana taimako a kan gajiyawarmu, domin ba mu ma san abin da ya kamata mu roƙa ba, amma Ruhu da kansa yana yi mana roƙo da nishe-nishen da ba su hurtuwa. <sup>27</sup>Amma shi mai fayyace zukatanmu ya san abin da Ruhu yake niyya, domin bisa ga nufin Allah ne Ruhu yake yi wa tsarkaka roƙo.
elements.passageText2: <sup>26</sup>Haka kuwa Ruhu yake yi mana taimako cikin kāsawarmu. Ba mu san yadda ya kamata mu yi adduˈa ba, amma Ruhu da kansa yana roƙon Allah a madadinmu tare da nishe-nishe waɗanda kalmomi ba za su iya bayyana ba. <sup>27</sup>Allah kuma, wanda shi ne mai bincika zuciyar mutum, ya san duk nufin Ruhu, domin Ruhu yana yin roƙo a madadin tsarkaka bisa ga nufin Allah ne.
elements.passageText5: <sup>26</sup>In the same way, the Spirit helps us in our weakness, for we do not know how we should pray, but the Spirit himself intercedes for us with inexpressible groanings. <sup>27</sup>And he who searches our hearts knows the mind of the Spirit, because the Spirit intercedes on behalf of the saints according to God’s will.

elements.question3: What does the Spirit do in these verses? What does this tell us about His nature as a person rather than a force?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: In these verses the Spirit helps us in our weakness, intercedes for us when we do not know how to pray, and does so according to God’s will — meaning He has knowledge of both our needs and God’s purposes. These are not the actions of an impersonal force. A force does not intercede, does not have a ’mind’, and does not groan on behalf of someone. Intercession requires awareness, compassion, intention, and relationship — all marks of personhood. The passage uses the personal pronoun ’himself’, reinforcing that the Spirit is a ’he’, not an ’it’.
elements.questionHint3: Think about what interceding for someone actually involves — could a wind or an electrical current do it? What does it require, and do those qualities belong to a force or a person?

elements.passageText1: <sup>30</sup>Ku kuma kula, kada fa ku ɓata wa Ruhu Mai Tsarki na Allah rai, wanda aka hatimce ku da shi, cewa ku nasa ne a ranar fansa.
elements.passageText2: <sup>30</sup>Ku kuma kula fa, kada ku ɓata wa Ruhu Mai Tsarki na Allah rai, gama shi alamar shaida ne na Allah a kanku da zai nuna a ranar fansa cewa ku nasa ne.
elements.passageText5: <sup>30</sup>And do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God, by whom you were sealed for the day of redemption.

elements.question3: Paul says we can ’grieve’ the Holy Spirit. What does that word tell us about the kind of being the Holy Spirit is?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Grief is an emotional response — it requires the capacity to feel, to care, and to be affected by what others do. You cannot grieve a force of nature: you cannot grieve electricity, or gravity, or the wind. The fact that Paul warns us not to grieve the Holy Spirit tells us that the Spirit has feelings and is personally affected by how we live. This places Him firmly in the category of a person, not a power. It also implies that the Spirit has a relationship with us close enough that our behaviour genuinely matters to Him.
elements.questionHint3: Can you grieve a tool, a machine, or a natural force? What kind of being must something be in order to experience grief, and what does that imply about the Holy Spirit?

elements.text3: What are some of the images used in the Bible to describe the Holy Spirit?

elements.passageText1: <sup>37</sup>7.37 L.Fir 23.36 A ranar ƙarshe ta idin, wato babbar ranar, sai Yesu ya tsaya, ya ɗaga murya ya ce, “Duk mai jin ƙishirwa yă zo gare ni yă sha. <sup>38</sup>7.38 Eze 47.1; Zak 14.8 Duk mai gaskatawa da ni, kamar yadda Nassi ya ce, ‘Daga zuciyarsa ne kogunan ruwan rai za su gudana.’ ” <sup>39</sup>To, wannan shi ne ya faɗa game da Ruhu, wanda masu gaskatawa da shi za su karɓa, domin har yanzu ba a ba da Ruhu ba, saboda ba a ɗaukaka Yesu ba tukuna.
elements.passageText2: <sup>37</sup>A ranar ƙarshe ta bikin, wadda kuma ta zama babbar rana ta bikin, Yesu ya tsaya ya ɗaga murya ya ce, “Duk mai jin ƙishin ruwa yă zo wurina yă sha. 7.37 L. Fir 23.36 <sup>38</sup>Duk mai ba da gaskiya gare ni, ‘Rafuffukan ruwa mai ba da rai za su fiffito daga zuciyarsa,’ kamar yadda Rubutacciyar Maganar Allah ta faɗa.” 7.37-38 Wani juyi na waɗannan ayoyin shi ne “Duk mai jin ƙishin ruwa yă zo wurina, kuma duk wanda ya ba da gaskiya gare ni kuma yă sha. 38. Kamar yadda Maganar Allah ta ce ‘rafuffukan ruwa mai ba da rai za su fiffito daga zuciyarsa.’ ” 7.38 Ezek 47.1; Zak 14.8 <sup>39</sup>Yesu yana magana ne a kan Ruhu Mai Tsarki, wanda masu ba da gaskiya gare shi za su karɓa, domin a wannan lokaci ba a ba da Ruhu Mai Tsarki ba tukuna, saboda ba a riga an ɗaukaka Yesu ba.
elements.passageText5: <sup>37</sup>On the last day of the feast, the greatest day, Jesus stood up and shouted out, ‘If anyone is thirsty, let him come to me, and <sup>38</sup>let the one who believes in me drink. Just as the scripture says, "From within him will flow rivers of living water."’ <sup>39</sup>(Now he said this about the Spirit, whom those who believed in him were going to receive, for the Spirit had not yet been given, because Jesus had not yet been glorified.)

elements.question3: Jesus uses the image of water to describe the Spirit. What does this image suggest about what the Spirit does in a person’s life?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Water is essential for life — without it, living things wither and die. By describing the Spirit as living water that flows from within, Jesus suggests that the Spirit is not an optional extra for believers but a vital, life-giving presence. The image of ’rivers’ flowing from within also suggests abundance and outward movement — the Spirit is not a small, stagnant pool but a generous, overflowing source. He satisfies a deep spiritual thirst that nothing else can meet, and His work in us is not contained — it spills outward to those around us.
elements.questionHint3: What happens to a person, plant, or animal that goes without water for a long time — and if the Spirit is like living water, what does that suggest about how essential He is to spiritual life?

elements.passageText1: <sup>2</sup>farat ɗaya, sai aka ji wani amo daga sama, kamar na busowar gawurtacciyar iska, ya cika duk gidan da suke zaune. <sup>3</sup>Sai waɗansu harsuna kamar na wuta suka bayyana a gare su, suna rarrabuwa, suna sassauka a kan ko wannensu.
elements.passageText2: <sup>2</sup>Ba labari sai aka ji wata ƙara daga sama kamar ta babbar iska, ta cika dukan gidan da suke zaune a ciki. <sup>3</sup>Sai ga waɗansu abubuwa kamar harsunan wuta, suka bayyana a gare su, suna rarrabuwa, suna sassauka a kan kowane ɗayansu. Coming of the Holy Spirit
elements.passageText5: <sup>2</sup>Suddenly a sound like a violent rushing wind came from heaven and filled the whole house where they were sitting. <sup>3</sup>And tongues spreading out like a fire appeared to them and came to rest on each one of them.

elements.question3: Wind and fire are the images here. What qualities do these suggest about the Holy Spirit’s arrival and work?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Wind and fire are both powerful, visible in their effects, and impossible to fully control or contain. The wind here is not a gentle breeze but a violent, rushing sound — suggesting the Spirit’s arrival was forceful, dramatic, and unmistakable. Fire is a purifying and consuming force, associated in the Old Testament with God’s presence. That tongues of fire rested on each individual suggests that the Spirit came not just to fill a room but to touch each person personally. Together, these images point to a Spirit whose coming is powerful, purifying, impossible to ignore, and deeply personal.
elements.questionHint3: Think about the properties of wind and fire — they are both powerful, both impossible to fully control, and both visible through their effects rather than directly. What might those qualities be saying about how the Spirit works?

elements.text3: To whom does God give His Spirit?

elements.passageText1: <sup>9</sup>Amma ku kam, ba masu zaman halin mutuntaka ba ne, masu zaman Ruhu ne, in dai har Ruhun Allah yana zaune a zuciyarku. Duk wanda ba shi da Ruhun Almasihu kuwa, ba na Almasihu ba ne.
elements.passageText2: <sup>9</sup>Ku kam ba rayuwar halin mutuntaka kuke yi ba, sai dai ta Ruhu, idan Ruhun Allah yana zaune a zukatanku. Duk wanda ba shi da Ruhun Almasihu kuwa, shi ba na Almasihu ba ne.
elements.passageText5: <sup>9</sup>You, however, are not in the flesh but in the Spirit, if indeed the Spirit of God lives in you. Now if anyone does not have the Spirit of Christ, this person does not belong to him.

elements.question3: According to Paul, what is the mark of belonging to Christ? What does this tell us about whether the Spirit is only for some Christians?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul says the mark of belonging to Christ is having the Spirit of Christ living in you. He then states the reverse just as clearly: if anyone does not have the Spirit, that person does not belong to Christ. There is no middle category — no such thing as a Christian without the Spirit. This means the Spirit is not a special gift for particularly advanced or committed Christians; He is given to every believer at the point of coming to faith. Receiving the Spirit is not a second step or a higher level of Christian experience — it is what it means to belong to Christ at all.
elements.questionHint3: Paul states this as an either/or with no exceptions — you either have the Spirit or you don’t belong to Christ. What does that absolute framing tell you about whether the Spirit is available only to certain kinds of Christians?

elements.text3: What do we receive from God through His Spirit?

elements.passageText1: <sup>8</sup>1.8 Mat 28.19; Mar 16.15; Luk 24.47,48 Amma za ku sami iko sa'ad da Ruhu Mai Tsarki ya sauko muku, za ku kuma zama shaiduna a Urushalima, da duk ƙasar Yahudiya, da ta Samariya, har ya zuwa iyakar duniya.”
elements.passageText2: <sup>8</sup>Amma za ku sami iko saˈad da Ruhu Mai Tsarki ya sauko muku, za ku zama shaiduna a Urushalima, da cikin dukan yankin Yahudiya, da Samariya, har zuwa iyakar duniya.” 1.8 Mat 28.19; Mar 16.15; Luk 24.47-48
elements.passageText5: <sup>8</sup>But you will receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you, and you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the farthest parts of the earth.

elements.question3: What does Jesus promise the Spirit will give us, and for what purpose? How does that apply to us today?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus promises that the Spirit will give us power, and He connects that power directly to a purpose: being His witnesses — starting locally and spreading outward to the ends of the earth. The power is not given for personal benefit but for mission. Today, the same promise applies to all believers. We are still called to be witnesses to Jesus in our homes, communities, cities, and across the world, and the Spirit is still the one who equips us for that. Without the Spirit’s power, effective witness is beyond us; with it, ordinary people can speak and act in ways that change lives.
elements.questionHint3: Jesus links the gift of power directly to a specific task — what is that task, and does the fact that it stretches ’to the farthest parts of the earth’ suggest it was only meant for the first disciples, or for all followers of Jesus?

elements.text3: How does the Holy Spirit help us as Christians?

elements.passageText1: <sup>7</sup>Duk da haka ina gaya muku gaskiya, zai fiye muku in tafi, domin in ban tafi ba, Mai Taimakon nan ba zai zo gare ku ba. In kuwa na tafi, zan aiko shi gare ku. <sup>8</sup>Sa'ad da kuwa ya zo zai faɗakar da duniya a kan zunubi, da adalci, da kuma hukunci. <sup>9</sup>Wato a kan zunubi, domin ba su gaskata da ni ba,
elements.passageText2: <sup>7</sup>Amma, ina faɗa muku gaskiya, tafiyata ta fi muku amfani, domin Mai Taimakon nan ba zai zo ba, idan ban tafi ba. Amma idan na tafi zan aiko muku da shi. <sup>8</sup>Lokacin da Mai Taimakon zai zo, zai tabbatar wa duniya cewa tunaninsu ba daidai ba ne a kan zunubi, da a kan adalci, da kuma a kan hukunci. <sup>9</sup>Tunaninsu a kan zunubi ba daidai ba ne saboda sun ƙi su kafa bangaskiyarsu gare ni.
elements.passageText5: <sup>7</sup>But I tell you the truth, it is to your advantage that I am going away. For if I do not go away, the Advocate will not come to you, but if I go, I will send him to you. <sup>8</sup>And when he comes, he will prove the world wrong concerning sin and righteousness and judgment— <sup>9</sup>concerning sin, because they do not believe in me;
elements.note: Advocate — or, in other versions, Helper, Comforter or Counsellor — is referring to the Holy Spirit.

elements.question3: Jesus says it is to our ’advantage’ that He goes away so the Advocate can come. Why might the Spirit’s presence be described as an advantage?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: When Jesus was on earth, His physical presence was limited to one place at a time — only those near Him could hear Him, see Him, or be helped by Him. The Spirit, by contrast, is given to all believers everywhere simultaneously. Every Christian in every century and every corner of the world has access to the Spirit’s help, teaching, and comfort — not just those who happened to walk the roads of Galilee. The Spirit also works from within rather than from outside, shaping our hearts and minds in ways that a physical presence alongside us could not. In that sense, the Spirit’s coming genuinely is an advantage: He is more widely present, more intimately available, and more personally transformative.
elements.questionHint3: When Jesus was physically present, how many people could be with Him at once — and how does the Spirit’s way of being present with believers differ from that limitation?

elements.passageText1: <sup>25</sup>“Duk wannan na faɗa muku ne tun muna tare. <sup>26</sup>Amma Mai Taimako, Ruhu Mai Tsarki, wanda Uba zai aiko da sunana, shi ne zai koya muku kome, ya kuma tuna muku duk abin da na faɗa muku.
elements.passageText2: <sup>25</sup>“Duk wannan na faɗa muku ne tun ina tare da ku. <sup>26</sup>Amma Mai Taimako, wato Ruhu Mai Tsarki, wanda Uba zai aiko cikin sunana, shi ne zai koya muku kome, yă kuma tuna muku duk abin da na faɗa muku.
elements.passageText5: <sup>25</sup>’I have spoken these things to you while I am still with you. <sup>26</sup>But the Advocate, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, will teach you everything, and will cause you to remember everything I said to you.’

elements.question3: What two things does Jesus say the Holy Spirit will do for His followers? How do you experience either of these in your own life?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus says the Holy Spirit will teach His followers everything, and will cause them to remember what Jesus said. These are two distinct but related gifts: ongoing teaching that opens up understanding of God’s truth, and a Spirit-aided memory that brings Jesus’ words back to mind when they are needed. In practice, many Christians experience this when reading Scripture and finding that a familiar passage suddenly becomes alive and meaningful in a new way, or when a verse comes to mind at exactly the moment it is needed. Both gifts point to the Spirit as the one who makes God’s word personally real and applicable in daily life.
elements.questionHint3: Jesus mentions two separate things the Spirit will do — can you identify them both in verse 26, and think of a time when you may have experienced either of them while reading the Bible or facing a difficult situation?

elements.passageText1: <sup>10</sup>mu ne Allah ya bayyana wa, ta wurin Ruhu, domin Ruhu shi yake fayyace kome, har ma zurfafan al'amuran Allah. <sup>11</sup>Wane ne a cikin mutane ya san tunanin wani mutum, in ba ruhun shi mutumin ba? Haka kuma ba wanda ya san tunanin Allah, sai dai Ruhun Allah. <sup>12</sup>Mu kuwa ba ruhun duniya muka samu ba, sai dai Ruhu wanda yake daga wurin Allah, domin mu fahimci abubuwan da Allah ya yi mana baiwa hannu sake. <sup>13</sup>Su ne kuwa muke sanarwa ta maganar da ba hikimar ɗan adam ce ta koyar ba, sai dai wadda Ruhu ya koyar, muna bayyana al'amura masu ruhu ga waɗanda suke na ruhu. <sup>14</sup>Mutumin da ba shi da Ruhu yakan ƙi yin na'am da al'amuran Ruhun Allah, don wauta ne a gare shi, ba kuwa zai iya fahimtarsu ba, domin ta wurin ruhu ne ake rarrabewa da su. <sup>15</sup>Mutumin da yake na ruhu kuwa yakan rarrabe da kome, shi kansa kuma ba mai jarraba shi.
elements.passageText2: <sup>10</sup>Amma Allah ya nuna mana waɗannan abubuwa ta wurin Ruhu, gama Ruhu shi yake bincike kome, har ma abubuwan Allah masu wuyar ganewa. <sup>11</sup>Wane ne zai iya sanin tunanin da yake a zuciyar wani mutum idan dai ba ruhun mutumin ba? Haka kuwa yake, ba wanda zai gane tunanin Allah, sai dai Ruhun Allah. <sup>12</sup>Mu kuwa ba ruhun duniya muka karɓo ba, sai dai Ruhu wanda yake daga wurin Allah, domin mu gane abubuwan da Allah ya ba mu hannu sake. <sup>13</sup>Muna kuma gaya muku waɗannan abubuwa, ba da kalmomin da ake koyarwa ta hikimar mutum ba, sai dai ta Ruhu, ta wurin bayyana abubuwan Ruhu ga waɗanda suke na Ruhu. 2.13 ga waɗanda suke na Ruhu: Da Helenanci an rubuta “a cikin kalmomin ruhaniya” ne a nan. <sup>14</sup>Mutumin da ba Ruhu ne yake bi da shi ba, ba ya karɓar abubuwan Ruhun Allah, gama a gare shi wawanci ne. Ba kuwa zai iya gane su ba, gama ta wurin Ruhu ne ake ganewa. <sup>15</sup>Mutumin da Ruhu yake bi da shi kuwa, yana iya gwada kome amma ba wanda zai iya gwada shi.
elements.passageText5: <sup>10</sup>God has revealed these things to us by the Spirit, for the Spirit searches all things, even the deep things of God. <sup>11</sup>For who among men knows the things of a man except the man’s spirit within him? So too, no one knows the things of God except the Spirit of God. <sup>12</sup>Now we have not received the spirit of the world, but the Spirit who is from God, so that we may know the things that are freely given to us by God. <sup>13</sup>And we speak about these things, not with words taught us by human wisdom, but with those taught by the Spirit, explaining spiritual things to spiritual people. <sup>14</sup>Now the unbeliever does not receive the things of the Spirit of God, for they are foolishness to him. And he cannot understand them, because they are spiritually discerned. <sup>15</sup>The one who is spiritual discerns all things, yet he himself is understood by no one.

elements.question3: According to Paul, what unique role does the Spirit play in helping us understand God and His word?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul argues that just as only a person’s own spirit truly knows what is going on inside them, only God’s Spirit truly knows the deep things of God. This means that genuine understanding of God and His word is not achievable through human intelligence or study alone — it requires the Spirit’s illumination. The Spirit, who knows God from the inside, reveals these things to believers. This is why Paul says the unbeliever finds spiritual things foolish: without the Spirit, they simply cannot be discerned. For the Christian, the Spirit is the one who makes God’s truth comprehensible, personal, and transformative — turning words on a page into living revelation.
elements.questionHint3: Paul uses an analogy about knowing another person’s inner thoughts — only their own spirit has access to that. How does he apply that same logic to the question of who can truly understand the things of God?

elements.passageText1: <sup>26</sup>Haka kuma Ruhu yake yi mana taimako a kan gajiyawarmu, domin ba mu ma san abin da ya kamata mu roƙa ba, amma Ruhu da kansa yana yi mana roƙo da nishe-nishen da ba su hurtuwa.
elements.passageText2: <sup>26</sup>Haka kuwa Ruhu yake yi mana taimako cikin kāsawarmu. Ba mu san yadda ya kamata mu yi adduˈa ba, amma Ruhu da kansa yana roƙon Allah a madadinmu tare da nishe-nishe waɗanda kalmomi ba za su iya bayyana ba.
elements.passageText5: <sup>26</sup>In the same way, the Spirit helps us in our weakness, for we do not know how we should pray, but the Spirit himself intercedes for us with inexpressible groanings.

elements.question3: Have you ever struggled to know how or what to pray? What comfort does this verse offer?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Most Christians experience times when they feel too overwhelmed, confused, or exhausted to pray — or simply do not know what to ask for. This verse offers real comfort by telling us that in precisely those moments, we are not left alone. The Spirit Himself steps in and intercedes for us, bringing before God what we cannot find words for. The phrase ’inexpressible groanings’ captures those moments of deep need that go beyond language. The comfort is not that prayer becomes easier, but that our inadequacy in prayer is covered — the Spirit carries what we cannot carry, and God, who searches our hearts, understands perfectly what the Spirit is expressing on our behalf.
elements.questionHint3: Paul doesn’t say the Spirit helps us find better words — he says the Spirit intercedes when we have no words at all. What does it mean for your prayer life to know that your weakness in prayer is something the Spirit is already covering?

elements.text3: What are the gifts of the Spirit?

elements.passageText1: <sup>8</sup>Wani an yi masa baiwa da koyar da hikima ta wurin Ruhu, wani kuma da koyar da sani ta wurin wannan Ruhu. <sup>9</sup>Wani kuwa an yi masa baiwa da bangaskiya ta wurin Ruhun nan, wani kuma baiwar warkarwa ta wurin Ruhun nan, <sup>10</sup>wani kuma yin mu'ujizai, wani kuma annabci, wani kuma baiwar rarrabe ruhu da aljani, wani kuma iya harshe iri iri, wani kuma fassara harsuna.
elements.passageText2: <sup>8</sup>Wani an ba shi baiwar koyar da hikima ta wurin Ruhu Mai Tsarki, wani kuma an yi masa baiwar koyar da sani ta wurin wannan Ruhu. <sup>9</sup>Wani kuma an yi masa baiwar bangaskiya ta wurin Ruhun nan, wani kuma baiwar warkewa ta wurin Ruhun nan, <sup>10</sup>wani kuma baiwar yin ayyukan ban mamaki, wani kuma annabci, wani kuma iya bambanta tsakanin ruhohi, wani kuma iya magana da harsuna dabam-dabam, wani kuma iya fassara harsuna.
elements.passageText5: <sup>8</sup>For to one is given a message of wisdom through the Spirit, and to another a message of knowledge by the same Spirit, <sup>9</sup>to another faith by the same Spirit, and to another gifts of healing by the one Spirit, <sup>10</sup>and to another performance of miracles, to another prophecy, and to another discernment of spirits, to another different kinds of tongues, and to another the interpretation of tongues.

elements.question3: List the gifts Paul mentions here. Are any of them surprising to you?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul lists: a message of wisdom, a message of knowledge, faith, gifts of healing, performance of miracles, prophecy, discernment of spirits, different kinds of tongues, and interpretation of tongues. The list spans both the seemingly ordinary (wisdom, knowledge, faith) and the more dramatic (miracles, tongues, healing). Many people are surprised to see ’faith’ listed as a gift — we might assume everyone has it equally — but Paul suggests that some are given an unusually strong or specific gift of faith. Others find the more supernatural gifts surprising, either because they did not expect them in the Bible, or because they had assumed they were no longer given today.
elements.questionHint3: Look back through the list carefully — which of these gifts would you expect to find in any church today, and are there any that seem more unusual or unexpected to you, and why?

elements.passageText1: <sup>22</sup>Albarkar Ruhu kuwa ita ce ƙauna, da farin ciki, da salama, da haƙuri, da kirki, da nagarta, da aminci, <sup>23</sup>da tawali'u da kuma kamunkai. Masu yin irin waɗannan abubuwa, ba dama shari'a ta kama su. <sup>24</sup>Waɗanda kuwa suke su na Almasihu Yesu ne, sun gicciye halin mutuntaka da mugayen sha'awace-sha'awace iri iri. <sup>25</sup>In dai rayuwar tamu ta Ruhu ce, to, sai mu tafiyar da al'amuranmu ta wurin ikon Ruhu.
elements.passageText2: <sup>22</sup>Amma halin da Ruhun Allah yake haifar shi ne ƙauna, da farin ciki, da salama, da haƙuri, da kirki, da nagarta, da aminci, <sup>23</sup>da tawaliˈu, da kuma shan ƙarfin shaˈawar jiki. Ai, babu dokar da ta hana waɗannan abubuwa. <sup>24</sup>Kuma dukan waɗanda suke na Almasihu Yesu sun gicciye halin mutuntaka da mugayen abubuwa iri-iri da jiki yake marmarinsu. <sup>25</sup>Tun da yake Ruhu ne ya ba mu rai, bari Ruhu yă bi da mu kuma cikin dukan zamanmu.
elements.passageText5: <sup>22</sup>But the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, <sup>23</sup>gentleness, and self-control. Against such things there is no law. <sup>24</sup>Now those who belong to Christ have crucified the flesh with its passions and desires. <sup>25</sup>If we live by the Spirit, let us also behave in accordance with the Spirit.

elements.question3: Paul calls these qualities ’fruit’ rather than ’gifts’. What does that distinction suggest about how they develop in a Christian’s life?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Gifts are given instantly and can be used immediately. Fruit, by contrast, grows gradually — it requires time, the right conditions, and a living connection to the source. By calling these qualities ’fruit of the Spirit’, Paul suggests they are not switched on overnight but develop slowly and organically as we remain connected to the Spirit and walk in step with Him. Just as you cannot force fruit to ripen by willpower alone, you cannot manufacture love, patience, or self-control by trying harder — they are the natural result of the Spirit’s life growing within us over time.
elements.questionHint3: Think about how fruit grows on a tree — does it appear instantly, or does it take time and depend on the health of the connection between the branch and the tree? How might that process mirror the way these qualities develop in a Christian?

elements.text3: For whose benefit are the gifts given?

elements.passageText1: <sup>7</sup>An yi wa kowanne baiwa da wani buɗi na Ruhu, don kyautata wa duka. ... <sup>11</sup>Dukan waɗannan Ruhu ɗaya ne da yake iza su, yake kuma rarraba wa ko wannensu yadda ya nufa.
elements.passageText2: <sup>7</sup>Allah yana ba kowane mutum bayyanar kasancewar Ruhu Mai Tsarki, domin amfanin kowa. ... <sup>11</sup>Dukan waɗannan Ruhu ɗaya ne yake yinsu, yake kuma rarraba wa kowa yadda yake so. 12.4-11 Rom 12.6-8
elements.passageText5: <sup>7</sup>To each person the manifestation of the Spirit is given for the benefit of all. ... <sup>11</sup>It is one and the same Spirit, distributing as he decides to each person, who produces all these things.

elements.question3: Who decides which gifts each person receives, and who are they ultimately for? How should that shape the way we use our gifts?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul says it is the Spirit who decides which gift each person receives — it is His sovereign choice, not ours to demand or select. And verse 7 makes clear that every gift is given ’for the benefit of all’, not for personal prestige or private use. This should shape the way we use our gifts significantly: if the Spirit chose them for the community’s benefit, then hoarding them, using them for self-promotion, or refusing to use them at all is a failure to fulfil their purpose. Our gifts are entrusted to us for others — they are a form of stewardship, not ownership.
elements.questionHint3: Verse 7 says gifts are given ’for the benefit of all’ — so if a gift is being used primarily to make the person look impressive or feel special, does that align with the purpose Paul describes here?

elements.text3: What is the Holy Spirit’s ultimate purpose?

elements.passageText1: <sup>14</sup>Zai ɗaukaka ni, domin zai ɗauko daga cikin abin da yake nawa ya sanar da ku.
