elements.passageText5: <sup>14</sup>He will glorify me, because he will receive from me what is mine and will tell it to you.

elements.question3: What does Jesus say the Spirit’s ultimate purpose is? How does this help us evaluate whether something is genuinely the Spirit’s work?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus says the Spirit’s ultimate purpose is to glorify Him — to take what belongs to Jesus and make it known. The Spirit does not draw attention to Himself or promote His own agenda; He points consistently to Jesus. This gives us a useful test for evaluating whether something is genuinely the Spirit’s work: does it exalt Jesus, or does it exalt something else — a personality, an experience, an institution? Where the Spirit is truly at work, Jesus becomes more central, more clearly known, and more deeply loved. Conversely, where the focus drifts away from Jesus, that is a signal to be cautious.
elements.questionHint3: If the Spirit’s stated purpose is to glorify Jesus, what question might you ask about a teaching, experience, or movement to test whether it is genuinely the Spirit’s work?

elements.question: What comes to mind when you hear the word ’Trinity’? Think about it before tapping ’Find out’.
elements.answer: Many people find the Trinity confusing — even Christians. The word itself doesn’t appear in the Bible, which can add to the uncertainty. What the Bible does teach is that there is one God, who exists as three persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. These three are distinct — they are not simply three ’modes’ or masks that God wears at different times — yet they are one God, not three. The Trinity is not a contradiction, but it is a mystery beyond full human understanding. What we can say is that the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit share the same divine nature and work together in everything God does.

elements.text3: Key truths

The Holy Spirit is a person, the third person of the Godhead.

"...Therefore go and make disciples of all nations, baptising them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit..." — Matthew 28:19

The Holy Spirit has been given to all believers, to show that they are God’s people.

"He anointed us, set his seal of ownership on us, and put his Spirit in our hearts as a deposit, guaranteeing what is to come." — 2 Corinthians 1:21b-22

elements.text3: Questions about creation come up whenever people discuss whether God exists. There is far more to explore here than one study can cover, but what matters most is what God Himself says about it. So in this study we go to the Biblical account — and it has more to say than you might expect.
Who was involved in the Creation?

elements.question3: What do Genesis 1:1 and Isaiah 45:12 tell us about God the Father’s role in creation?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Both passages present God as the sole Creator and originator of everything that exists. Genesis 1:1 states this simply and absolutely — ‘In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth’ — leaving no room for creation to have happened without Him or before Him. Isaiah 45:12 adds a note of personal directness: God speaks in the first person, saying ’I made the earth’ and ’my own hands stretched out the heavens’. He did not delegate creation to another power; He made it Himself, with full authority — even giving orders to the stars. The overall picture is of a God who is the personal, sovereign, and intentional originator of all that exists.
elements.questionHint3: In Isaiah 45:12, God uses the words ’I’ and ’my own hands’ — what does that personal, direct language suggest about how involved He was in the act of creation, and whether He needed anyone else’s help?

elements.question3: What do John 1:1-3 and Colossians 1:16-17 reveal about Jesus’ role in creation? Does this change how you think about Him?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: John 1:1-3 tells us that nothing was created apart from Jesus — He was not a created being but the one through whom all creation came into existence. Colossians 1:16-17 deepens this further: all things were created not just through Him but by Him and for Him — meaning the entire created order has Jesus as both its origin and its goal. Verse 17 then adds that He is ’before all things’ and that in Him all things ’hold together’, meaning the universe continues to exist and cohere because of His ongoing sustaining power. For many people, thinking of Jesus primarily as a teacher or healer, this picture of Him as the Creator and Sustainer of all things significantly changes how they understand who He is.
elements.questionHint3: Colossians 1:16 says everything was created not just ’through’ Jesus but also ’for’ him — what does it mean for creation to have been made for Jesus, and how does that compare with the way you normally think about Him?

elements.question3: What do Genesis 1:2 and Psalm 104:30 reveal about the Holy Spirit’s role in creation? What does it mean that all three persons of the Trinity were involved?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Genesis 1:2 places the Spirit of God at the very beginning — moving over the formless, empty waters before God speaks creation into order. The image suggests a hovering, active presence, ready and involved from the outset. Psalm 104:30 shows that the Spirit’s creative work is not limited to the initial act: when God sends His breath (Spirit), living creatures are created and the ground is renewed. The Spirit is involved in ongoing creation and replenishment, not just in the original event. The involvement of all three persons of the Trinity — Father, Son, and Spirit — in creation tells us that creation was not the work of one member acting alone, but a unified act of the whole Godhead. The world did not come from one dimension of God but from the fullness of who God is.
elements.questionHint3: Genesis 1:2 shows the Spirit present at the very start, before anything was formed — and Psalm 104:30 shows Him involved in bringing new life into being. What do those two moments together suggest about the scope of the Spirit’s involvement in creation?

elements.text3: What did God create?

elements.question: It’s worth reading the whole passage before answering the question below…
elements.answer:
1In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth. 2Now the earth was without shape and empty, and darkness was over the surface of the watery deep, but the Spirit of God was moving over the surface of the water. 3God said, "Let there be light." And there was light! 4God saw that the light was good, so God separated the light from the darkness. 5God called the light "day" and the darkness "night." There was evening, and there was morning, marking the first day.

6God said, "Let there be an expanse in the midst of the waters and let it separate water from water." 7So God made the expanse and separated the water under the expanse from the water above it. It was so. 8God called the expanse "sky." There was evening, and there was morning, a second day.

9God said, "Let the water under the sky be gathered to one place and let dry ground appear." It was so. 10God called the dry ground "land" and the gathered waters he called "seas." God saw that it was good. 11God said, "Let the land produce vegetation: plants yielding seeds and trees on the land bearing fruit with seed in it, according to their kinds." It was so. 12The land produced vegetation—plants yielding seeds according to their kinds, and trees bearing fruit with seed in it according to their kinds. God saw that it was good. 13There was evening, and there was morning, a third day.

14God said, "Let there be lights in the expanse of the sky to separate the day from the night, and let them be signs to indicate seasons and days and years, 15and let them serve as lights in the expanse of the sky to give light on the earth." It was so. 16God made two great lights—the greater light to rule over the day and the lesser light to rule over the night. He made the stars also. 17God placed the lights in the expanse of the sky to shine on the earth, 18to preside over the day and the night, and to separate the light from the darkness. God saw that it was good. 19There was evening, and there was morning, a fourth day.

20God said, "Let the water swarm with swarms of living creatures and let birds fly above the earth across the expanse of the sky." 21God created the great sea creatures and every living and moving thing with which the water swarmed, according to their kinds, and every winged bird according to its kind. God saw that it was good. 22God blessed them and said, "Be fruitful and multiply and fill the water in the seas, and let the birds multiply on the earth." 23There was evening, and there was morning, a fifth day.

24God said, "Let the land produce living creatures according to their kinds: cattle, creeping things, and wild animals, each according to their kind." It was so. 25God made the wild animals according to their kinds, the cattle according to their kinds, and all the creatures that creep along the ground according to their kinds. God saw that it was good.

26Then God said, "Let us make humankind in our image, after our likeness, so they may rule over the fish of the sea and the birds of the air, over the cattle, and over all the earth, and over all the creatures that move on the earth." 27God created humankind in his own image, in the image of God he created them, male and female he created them.

28God blessed them and said to them, "Be fruitful and multiply! Fill the earth and subdue it! Rule over the fish of the sea and the birds of the air and every creature that moves on the ground."

31God saw all that he had made—and it was very good! There was evening, and there was morning, the sixth day.

2:1The heavens and the earth were completed with everything that was in them. 2By the seventh day God finished the work that he had been doing, and he ceased on the seventh day all the work that he had been doing. 3God blessed the seventh day and made it holy because on it he ceased all the work that he had been doing in creation.


(NET - see context ↗)

elements.question3: From Genesis 1:1–2:3, list six main things God created. What pattern or structure do you notice across the six days?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Six main things God created: (1) light and the separation of day and night; (2) the sky and the separation of waters; (3) dry land, seas, and vegetation; (4) the sun, moon, and stars; (5) sea creatures and birds; (6) land animals and human beings. A clear pattern emerges across the six days: the first three days form the environments or ’spaces’ (light, sky, land/sea), and the next three days fill those spaces with their corresponding inhabitants (luminaries fill the sky, fish and birds fill sea and sky, animals and humans fill the land). Each day ends with ’God saw that it was good’, and the whole sequence concludes with ’very good’. The pattern moves from formless and empty to ordered and filled — showing creation as deliberate, structured, and purposeful.
elements.questionHint3: Look at days 1-3 and then days 4-6 side by side — do you notice a relationship between what is created on day 1 and day 4, day 2 and day 5, day 3 and day 6? What does that pairing suggest about the structure of the account?

elements.text3: How did God create the world?

elements.question3: God creates simply by speaking. What does this tell us about His power and authority over the created world?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The repeated pattern of ’God said... and it was so’ throughout Genesis 1 shows that God’s word alone is sufficient to bring anything into existence. He does not need materials, effort, tools, or assistance — He simply speaks and reality responds instantly and completely. This tells us that God’s authority over creation is absolute: the created world does not exist independently of Him but is entirely subject to His command. No human being, no force of nature, and nothing in the universe has power comparable to God’s — creation came into being by His word, continues to exist by His sustaining power, and answers to Him entirely.
elements.questionHint3: When a human being gives an order, it might or might not be obeyed — but when God speaks in Genesis 1, what always happens immediately, and what does that instant, total response tell you about the kind of authority He has?

elements.text3: What did God think about everything He had created?

elements.question3: God declares His creation ’very good’. What does that tell us about the world as God originally made it — and why does that matter for how we think about the world now?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The declaration ’very good’ tells us that the world as God originally made it was not flawed, broken, or morally neutral — it was perfectly aligned with God’s intention and reflected His character. There was no suffering, no death, no corruption. This matters enormously for how we think about the world now, because it means the pain, brokenness, and evil we see are not original features of creation — they are intrusions. The world as it now is has been damaged, not designed that way. This gives us reason to grieve what has been lost and hope for what God has promised to restore — it also means we cannot blame God for the state of the world as it currently exists.
elements.questionHint3: If God called the original creation ’very good’ but the world we see today contains suffering and brokenness, what does that difference suggest about whether God designed things this way, or whether something has gone wrong since?

elements.text3: Why did God create the world?

elements.passageText2: <sup>1</sup>Sammai suna shelar
elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>The heavens declare the glory of God; the sky displays his handiwork.

elements.question3: According to this verse, what purpose does creation serve? Can you think of a moment when the natural world has pointed you towards God?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: According to this verse, creation serves the purpose of declaring God’s glory and displaying His handiwork. The heavens are not silent witnesses — they actively proclaim something about their Maker. Creation is, in a sense, a constant, universal testimony to God’s existence, power, and beauty, available to every person in every age and culture. Many people find that moments in the natural world — a vast night sky, a mountain range, a coastline, the intricacy of a living creature — produce a sense of awe that points instinctively beyond the thing itself to the one who made it. This is precisely what the psalmist says creation is designed to do.
elements.questionHint3: The psalm says the heavens ’declare’ something — as if creation is actively communicating a message. What is the message it is declaring, and have you ever found yourself sensing that message when looking at something in the natural world?

elements.passageText2: <sup>7</sup>Ku kawo duk wanda aka san shi da Sunana,

elements.question3: Isaiah says God formed and made us for His glory. What does it mean to live for God’s glory in practice?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: To live for God’s glory means to live in a way that makes God look great — that draws attention to His character, His goodness, and His worth rather than to ourselves. In practice, this is not about a single dramatic act but about the orientation of an entire life: the choices we make, the way we treat people, the things we pursue and prioritise, and whether our lives point others towards God or away from Him. Since we are made in God’s image (Genesis 1:27), living for His glory also means reflecting His character — His love, justice, faithfulness, and compassion — in our everyday lives. The purpose of our existence, according to Isaiah, is not self-fulfilment but God-centred living.
elements.questionHint3: If being created ’for God’s glory’ means our lives are meant to point to Him, what kinds of things might a person do — or avoid doing — in ordinary daily life that would either reflect or contradict that purpose?

elements.text3: Why did God create human beings?

elements.passageText1: <sup>27</sup>1.27 Far 5.1,2 1.27 Mat 19.4; Mar 10.6 Haka nan fa, Allah ya halicci mutum cikin siffarsa, cikin siffar Allah, Allah ya halicci mutum, namiji da ta mace ya halicce su.

elements.passageText5: <sup>27</sup>God created mankind in his own image, in the image of God he created them, male and female he created them.

elements.question3: What does it mean that human beings are made ’in the image of God’? How does this set us apart from the rest of creation?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: Being made in the image of God means that human beings uniquely reflect something of God’s own nature — His rationality, His moral sense, His capacity for relationship, creativity, and love. No other creature in Genesis is described this way. The animals are made ’according to their kinds’, but human beings are made according to God’s own image and likeness, which places us in an entirely different category. It means we are not merely biological organisms but personal beings designed for relationship with God, capable of knowing Him, representing Him in the world, and being held morally accountable to Him. It also means that every human life has an inherent dignity that cannot be stripped away, because it is God’s image that is borne in each person.
elements.questionHint3: Genesis describes animals being made ’according to their kinds’ but describes humans differently — made in the image and likeness of God Himself. What does that different description suggest about the kind of relationship God intends to have with human beings compared to the rest of creation?

elements.text3: The fact that we are made in God’s image sets human beings apart from the rest of creation. It means we are not simply the product of chance or biology — we are creatures made for relationship with God, capable of reflecting His character in the way we live.
What responsibility has God given human beings?

elements.passageText5: <sup>15</sup>The Lord God took the man and placed him in the orchard in Eden to care for it and to maintain it.

elements.question3: What two responsibilities do these verses give to human beings in relation to God’s creation? How well do you think we are fulfilling them today?
elements.answerPlaceholder3: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer3: The two responsibilities are: (1) to rule over creation — exercising authority and oversight on God’s behalf as His representatives in the world; and (2) to care for and maintain it — the language of Genesis 2:15 is that of a gardener or steward, tending and preserving what has been entrusted. These are complementary, not contradictory: the authority to rule is given in the context of responsible care, not exploitation. As for how well we are fulfilling these responsibilities today — many would argue honestly that the widespread environmental damage, species loss, and exploitation of natural resources reflects a failure of the stewardship God intended. The mandate to rule was never a licence to destroy; it was a call to serve creation on God’s behalf.
elements.questionHint3: Genesis 1:26 gives humans authority to ’rule over’ creation, while Genesis 2:15 says the man was placed in the garden to ’care for it and maintain it’ — do those two ideas feel like the same thing to you, or do they add different dimensions to what responsible stewardship looks like?

elements.text3: Key truths

When God made the world, He declared that it was ’very good’; indeed, it was perfect.

"God saw all that he had made—and it was very good! There was evening, and there was morning, the sixth day." — Genesis 1:31

We are made in God’s image.

"So God created mankind in his own image, in the image of God he created them; male and female he created them." — Genesis 1:27
To think about

How can we look after God’s creation?

elements.text3: In the last study we saw that the world God made was very good — perfect. So what went wrong? In this study we look at sin: where it came from, what it does to us, and how God responds to it.
What state is the world in now?

elements.question3: What picture does the writer of Ecclesiastes paint of life under the sun? What is your reaction to his description?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The writer paints a picture of life as fundamentally empty and futile — all human activity and striving amounts to ’chasing after wind’, which is a vivid image for pursuing something that can never be caught or held. Verse 15 deepens the bleakness: what is bent cannot be straightened, and what is missing cannot even be fully counted. In other words, the world is broken in ways that human effort cannot fix and human wisdom cannot fully diagnose. Many people find this description uncomfortably honest — it matches the nagging sense that however much we achieve, something remains deeply unsatisfying about life lived without reference to God.
elements.questionHint3: The phrase ’chasing after wind’ describes trying to catch something — what is the point the writer is making about whether that chase can ever succeed, and do you think that rings true of how people experience life?

elements.passageText1: <sup>7</sup>Ba dama duniya ta ƙi ku, amma ni take ƙi, don na shaide ta a kan ayyukanta mugaye ne.
elements.passageText2: <sup>7</sup>Ku kam duniya ba za ta iya ƙinku ba, amma ni take ƙi, domin ina faɗa mata mugayen ayyukan da take yi.
elements.passageText5: <sup>7</sup>The world cannot hate you, but it hates me, because I testify about it that its deeds are evil.

elements.question3: What does Jesus say about the deeds of the world? Why do you think the world reacted to him with hatred?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus says the world’s deeds are evil, and that this honest testimony is precisely the reason the world hates Him. He is not speaking as someone who hates the world, but as one who tells the truth about it — and the truth is unwelcome. The hatred is a reaction to exposure: people who are comfortable with their way of living do not want someone to name it as evil. This is a pattern we recognise: we tend to dislike people who hold a mirror up to behaviour we would rather not examine. The world’s hostility to Jesus is therefore itself a kind of evidence that His assessment is accurate.
elements.questionHint3: Jesus says the world hates Him specifically because He testifies about its deeds — why might telling the truth about someone’s behaviour cause them to react with hostility rather than gratitude?

elements.passageText1: <sup>19</sup>Ga shi, duk halitta tana marmari, tana ɗokin ganin bayyanar 'ya'yan Allah. <sup>20</sup>8.20 Far 3.17-19 An sarayar da halitta ta zama banza, ba da nufinta ba, amma da nufin wanda ya sarayar da ita. Duk da haka, akwai sa zuciya, <sup>21</sup>gama za a 'yantar da halitta da kanta ma, daga bautar ruɓewa, domin ta sami 'yancin nan, na ɗaukaka na 'ya'yan Allah. <sup>22</sup>Mun dai san dukan halitta tana nishi, na shan azaba kuma irin ta mai naƙuda, har ya zuwa yanzu.
elements.passageText2: <sup>19</sup>Dukan halitta tana marmari sosai, tana jiran Allah yă bayyana ˈyaˈyansa. <sup>20</sup>Gama an sa halitta ta kāsa kai ga manufarta, wannan kuwa ba bisa ga nufin halitta da kanta ba ne, sai dai domin haka ne Allah ya so. An yi wannan da sa zuciya 8.20 Far 3.17-19 <sup>21</sup>cewa halitta da kanta za ta sami tsira daga bautar da take wa ruɓewa, za ta kuma sami ˈyancin nan na ɗaukakar da za a yi wa ˈyaˈyan Allah. <sup>22</sup>Mun dai san cewa har wa yau dukan halitta tana nishi kamar yadda mace mai jin zafin haifuwa takan yi.
elements.passageText5: <sup>19</sup>For the creation eagerly waits for the revelation of the sons of God. <sup>20</sup>For the creation was subjected to futility—not willingly, but because of the one who subjected it—in hope <sup>21</sup>that the creation itself will also be set free from the bondage of decay into the glorious liberty of the children of God. <sup>22</sup>For we know that the whole creation groans and suffers together until now.

elements.question3: What has happened to creation as a result of sin? What does Paul say creation is waiting for?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul says that the whole of creation has been subjected to futility and is in bondage to decay — it is not functioning as it was originally intended to. The language of groaning and suffering captures the brokenness we see in the natural world: disease, death, disaster, and deterioration. Crucially, Paul says this happened not willingly but as a consequence of human sin — creation was dragged into the Fall. However, the passage is not without hope: creation is not just suffering, it is waiting and longing. It eagerly anticipates the day when God’s children are fully revealed and creation itself is set free from decay into a new kind of glorious freedom.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says creation was subjected to futility ’not willingly’ — what does that tell you about whether the brokenness in the natural world is creation’s own fault or a consequence of something human beings did?

elements.text3: How does the Bible describe sin?

elements.passageText1: <sup>6</sup>53.6 1Bit 2.25 Dukanmu muna kama da tumakin da suka ɓata,
elements.passageText2: <sup>6</sup>Dukanmu kama da tumakin da suka ɓata,
elements.passageText5: <sup>6</sup>All of us had wandered off like sheep; each of us had turned his own way. The Lord let the guilt of all of us fall on him.

elements.question3: How does Isaiah describe the nature of sin using the image of sheep? What does it mean to have ’turned his own way’?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Sheep are not known for dramatic rebellion — they wander. They do not storm off defiantly; they simply drift, following whatever attracts them in the moment, unaware of how far they have strayed from the shepherd and how much danger they are in. This is a telling image for sin: much of it is not dramatic wickedness but a gradual, unremarkable drifting away from God. To have ’turned his own way’ means each person has set their own course rather than following God’s — making themselves the authority on how to live. It is self-direction rather than God-direction, which the Bible understands as the essence of sin.
elements.questionHint3: Sheep don’t usually rebel dramatically — they just wander gradually without realising how lost they are becoming. How does that kind of slow drift compare to the way you might normally picture sin, and does it change your understanding of it?

elements.passageText1: <sup>43</sup>Sau da yawa Ubangiji yakan ceci jama'arsa,
elements.passageText2: <sup>43</sup>Sau da yawa Yahweh
elements.passageText5: <sup>43</sup>Many times he delivered them, but they had a rebellious attitude and were degraded by their sin.

elements.question3: What does this verse reveal about both the persistence of human sin and the persistence of God’s response?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The verse reveals that human sin is stubbornly persistent: despite being delivered ’many times’, the people returned again and again to a rebellious attitude. Sin is not merely a series of isolated bad choices — the verse describes an underlying disposition of rebellion that keeps reasserting itself. But the verse equally reveals that God’s response is just as persistent: He delivered them ’many times’, not once. He did not give up after the first failure, or the second, or the tenth. The pattern on the human side is repeated failure; the pattern on God’s side is repeated rescue. The verse also notes the degrading effect of sin — it diminishes the people, not just morally but in their whole condition.
elements.questionHint3: The verse says God delivered them ’many times’ — what does the word ’many’ suggest about how patient God was with their repeated failures, and what does the pattern of repeated rescue tell you about His character?

elements.text3: When did sin first come into the world?

elements.passageText1: <sup>16</sup>Ubangiji Allah ya yi wa mutumin umarni, ya ce, “Kana da 'yanci ka ci daga kowane itace da yake a gonar, <sup>17</sup>amma daga itacen sanin nagarta da mugunta ba za ka ci ba, gama a ranar da ka ci shi za ka mutu lalle.”
elements.passageText2: <sup>16</sup>Sai Yahweh Allah ya ce masa, “Kana da ˈyanci ka ci daga kowane itace na gonar, <sup>17</sup>amma daga Itace Mai Kawo Sanin Nagarta da Mugunta ba za ka ci ba, gama a ranar da ka ci daga itacen nan lallai za ka mutu.”
elements.passageText5: <sup>16</sup>Then the Lord God commanded the man, ‘You may freely eat fruit from every tree of the orchard, <sup>17</sup>but you must not eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, for when you eat from it you will surely die.’

elements.question3: What had God commanded, and what consequence did he attach to disobedience?
elements.sampleAnswer3: God gave a generous permission — the man could freely eat from every tree of the orchard — alongside a single clear prohibition: he must not eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil. The command was not restrictive in any harsh sense; the one forbidden tree was set against the backdrop of abundant provision. The consequence attached to disobedience was death: ‘you will surely die.’ This was not a threat but a statement of consequence — crossing this boundary would bring death into a world where it had not previously existed. The command established that God’s word was the boundary of life, and to step outside it was to step towards death.
elements.questionHint3: Before giving the one prohibition, God said the man could ’freely eat’ from every other tree — what does that generous framing tell you about the kind of God who was setting this boundary, and does that change how you read the single restriction?

elements.passageText1: <sup>1</sup>3.1 W.Yah 12.9; 20.2 Maciji ya fi kowace dabba da Ubangiji Allah ya yi wayo. Ya ce wa matar, “Ko Allah ya ce, ‘Ba za ku ci daga wani itacen da yake a gonar ba?’ ” <sup>2</sup>Sai matar ta ce wa macijin, “Mā iya ci daga cikin itatuwan gonar, <sup>3</sup>amma Allah ya ce, ‘Ba za ku ci daga cikin 'ya'yan itacen da yake tsakiyar gonar ba, ba za ku taɓa shi ba, don kada ku mutu.’ ” <sup>4</sup>Amma macijin ya ce wa matar, “Hakika ba za ku mutu ba. <sup>5</sup>Gama Allah ya sani a ranar da kuka ci daga itacen nan idanunku za su buɗe, za ku kuwa zama kamar Allah, ku san nagarta da mugunta.” <sup>6</sup>Matar ta ga yadda itacen yana da kyau, 'ya'yansa kuma kyawawa, abin sha'awa ne kuma ga ido, abin marmari ne domin ba da hikima, ta tsinka daga 'ya'yansa, ta kuwa ci, ta kuma bai wa mijinta waɗansu, shi kuma ya ci. <sup>7</sup>Sai dukansu biyu idanunsu suka buɗe, sa'an nan suka gane tsirara suke, sai suka samo ganyayen ɓaure suka ɗinɗinka suka yi wa kansu sutura.

Maciji ya ce wa macen, “Ko Allah ya ce, lallai ba za ku ci daga kowane itacen da yake a gonar ba?” 3.1 R. Yoh 12.9; 20.2 <sup>2</sup>Macen ta amsa wa macijin ta ce, “Muna da ˈyanci mu ci daga ˈyaˈyan itatuwan da suke a gonar. <sup>3</sup>Amma Allah ya ce, ‘Ba za ku ci daga ˈyaˈyan itacen da yake a tsakiyar gonar ba. Kada ku ci ko ku taɓa shi, domin kada ku mutu.’ ” <sup>4</sup>Amma maciji ya ce wa macen, “Ko kaɗan, ba za ku mutu ba. <sup>5</sup>Gama Allah ya sani cewa a ranar da kuka ci daga ˈyaˈyan itacen nan, idanunku za su buɗe. Za ku kuwa zama kamar Allah, masu sanin nagarta da mugunta.” <sup>6</sup>Saˈad da macen ta ga ˈyaˈyan itacen suna da kyau domin ci, abin shaˈawa ne ga idanu, kuma abin marmari ne domin samun hikima, sai ta tsinka ta ci. Ta kuma ba mijinta wanda yake tare da ita, shi ma ya ci. <sup>7</sup>Saˈan nan idanun kowannensu suka buɗe, suka gane cewa suna tsirara. Sai suka samo ganyayen ɓaure suka ɗinɗinka suka yi wa kansu abin rufe tsiraicinsu.
elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>Now the serpent was more shrewd than any of the wild animals the Lord God had made. He said to the woman, ‘Is it really true that God said, "You must not eat from any tree of the orchard"?’. . . <sup>6</sup>When the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was a delight to the eyes, and that the tree was desirable for imparting wisdom, she took some of its fruit and ate; and she also gave some to her husband who was with her, and he ate. <sup>7</sup>Then the eyes of both of them were opened, and they knew they were naked; so they sewed fig leaves together and made coverings for themselves.

elements.question3: What strategy did the serpent use to tempt the woman? What motivated her to eat the fruit?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The serpent’s strategy was to sow doubt about God’s word — beginning with a question that subtly distorted what God had actually said (‘Did God really say you must not eat from any tree?’), and then implying that God had an ulterior motive in giving the command. The tactic moved from questioning God’s word to questioning God’s goodness. The woman’s motivations for eating the fruit were threefold: she saw it was good for food (physical appetite), a delight to the eyes (aesthetic desire), and desirable for gaining wisdom (the ambition to be more). These three motivations — appetite, beauty, and pride — represent a recognisably human pattern of temptation that has continued ever since.
elements.questionHint3: The serpent’s first move was not to say ’disobey God’ but to ask a question that cast doubt on what God had said — why is making someone question the reliability of God’s word an effective first step towards getting them to disobey it?

elements.text3: What does sin involve? Where does it come from?

elements.passageText1: <sup>14</sup>Sai ya sāke kiran jama'a, yă ce musu, “Ku saurare ni dukanku, ku kuma fahimta. <sup>15</sup>Ba abin da yake shiga mutum daga waje yă ƙazanta shi, sai abin da yake fita, yake ƙazanta shi. <sup>16</sup>[Duk mai kunnen ji, yă ji.]” <sup>17</sup>Bayan rabuwarsa da taron, da ya shiga gida, almajiransa suka tambaye shi ma'anar misalin. <sup>18</sup>Ya ce musu, “Ashe, ku ma ba ku da fahimta ne? Ashe, ba ku gane ba, kome ya shiga mutum daga waje, ba yadda zai ƙazanta shi, <sup>19</sup>tun da yake ba zuciya tasa ya shiga ba, cikinsa ne, ta haka kuma zai fita?” Ta haka Yesu ya halatta kowane irin abinci. <sup>20</sup>Ya kuma ce, “Abin da yake fita daga cikin mutum yake ƙazanta shi. <sup>21</sup>Don daga ciki ne, wato daga zuciyar mutum, mugayen tunani yake fitowa, kamar su fasikanci, da sata, da kisankai, da zina, <sup>22</sup>da kwaɗayi, da mugunta, da ha'inci, da fajirci, da kishi, da yanke, da girmankai, da kuma wauta. <sup>23</sup>Duk waɗannan mugayen abubuwa daga ciki suke fitowa, suna kuwa ƙazanta mutum.”
elements.passageText2: <sup>14</sup>Sai Yesu ya sāke kiran jamaˈa, ya ce musu, “Ku saurare ni dukanku, ku kuma gane da kyau. <sup>15</sup>Abin da yake shiga cikin mutum daga waje ba shi ne yake ƙazantar da mutum a gaban Allah ba, abin da yake fitowa daga cikin mutum, shi ne yake ƙazantar da mutum a gaban Allah. <sup>16</sup>Duk mai kunnen ji, bari yă ji.” 7.16 Waɗansu rubuce-rubuce na dā ba su da wannan aya. <sup>17</sup>Saˈad da ya rabu da taron ya shiga gida, almajiransa suka tambaye shi maˈanar wannan koyarwa. <sup>18</sup>Ya amsa ya ce, “Ashe, ku ma ba ku gane ba? Ba ku gane cewa duk abin da ya shiga cikin mutum daga waje ba ya iya ƙazantar da mutum a gaban Allah, <sup>19</sup>gama ba cikin zuciyarsa yake shiga ba, amma cikin cikinsa ne, saˈan nan yă kuma fita daga jikinsa?” A wannan maganar Yesu ya nuna cewa kowane irin abinci marar ƙazanta ne. <sup>20</sup>Ya ci gaba da cewa, “Abin da yake fitowa daga cikin mutum, shi ne yake ƙazantar da mutum. <sup>21</sup>Don daga cikin zuciyar mutum ne mugayen tunani suke fitowa, kamar su lalata, da sata, da kisan kai, da zina, <sup>22</sup>da kwaɗayi, da mugunta, da ruɗu, da halin neman jin daɗi na rashin kunya, da kishi, da ɓata suna, da girman kai, da kuma wawanci. <sup>23</sup>Duk waɗannan mugayen abubuwa daga cikin zuciya suke fitowa, suna kuwa ƙazantar da mutum.”
elements.passageText5: <sup>14</sup>Then he called the crowd again and said to them, ‘Listen to me, everyone, and understand. <sup>15</sup>There is nothing outside of a person that can defile him by going into him. Rather, it is what comes out of a person that defiles him.’ . . . <sup>21</sup>For from within, out of the human heart, come evil ideas, sexual immorality, theft, murder, <sup>22</sup>adultery, greed, evil, deceit, debauchery, envy, slander, pride, and folly. <sup>23</sup>All these evils come from within and defile a person.’

elements.question3: According to Jesus, where does sin originate? How does this challenge the idea that our environment or upbringing is primarily responsible for our behaviour?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus locates the origin of sin firmly within the human heart. Evil does not primarily come into us from outside — it comes out of us from within. This directly challenges the popular idea that people are basically good and only made bad by difficult circumstances, poor upbringing, or a corrupt environment. While those factors may shape how sin expresses itself, Jesus says they are not its source. The source is the human heart itself. This is a sobering diagnosis because it means the problem cannot be solved simply by improving conditions, education, or social structures — the root is internal, and it requires an internal solution.
elements.questionHint3: Many people believe that if you could give everyone a good upbringing and a fair environment, people would naturally behave well — how does Jesus’ diagnosis in this passage agree or disagree with that assumption, and what are the implications either way?

elements.question: What comes to mind when you hear the word sin?
elements.answer: For many people, ‘sin’ brings to mind a list of particularly bad actions — murder, theft, serious crimes. By that measure, most of us feel fairly confident we are not sinners. But the Bible uses the word much more broadly. Sin is any thought, word, or action that falls short of God’s standard — and, more than that, it is an attitude of the heart that places self at the centre rather than God. It is rebellion against God’s authority over our lives, often in quiet, respectable ways. When we understand sin this way, the Bible’s claim that ’all have sinned’ (Romans 3:23) starts to make a great deal more sense.

elements.text3: How does God feel about sin?

elements.passageText1: <sup>5</sup>6.5 Mat 24.37; Luk 17.26; 1Bit 3.20 Ubangiji kuwa ya ga muguntar mutum ta ƙasaita a duniya, dukan zace-zacen tunanin zuciyarsa kuma mugunta ne kullayaumin. <sup>6</sup>Ubangiji ya damu da ya yi mutum a duniya, abin ya ɓata masa zuciya ƙwarai.
elements.passageText2: <sup>5</sup>Yahweh kuwa ya lura cewa muguntar ˈyan Adam ta yi yawa a duniya, kuma kowane tunanin zuciyarsu zallar mugunta ce kullum. 6.5 Mat 24.37; Luk 17.26; 1 Bit 3.20 <sup>6</sup>Yahweh kuwa ya yi baƙin ciki da ya yi ˈyan Adam a duniya, kuma zuciyarsa ta ɓaci ƙwarai.
elements.passageText5: <sup>5</sup>But the Lord saw that the wickedness of humankind had become great on the earth. Every inclination of the thoughts of their minds was only evil all the time. <sup>6</sup>The Lord regretted that he had made humankind on the earth, and he was highly offended.

elements.question3: How does God respond emotionally to human wickedness? What does this tell us about how seriously God takes sin?
elements.sampleAnswer3: God’s response is one of grief and deep offence — He regretted making humankind and was highly offended by what He saw. These are strong emotional terms that tell us sin is not a matter of indifference to God. He is not a detached observer who shrugs at human wickedness; He is personally grieved by it. This matters because it shows that sin is not primarily about breaking rules — it is about breaking relationship. God made human beings for closeness with Himself, and seeing them given over entirely to evil (‘only evil all the time’) causes Him genuine pain. The depth of His emotional response is a measure of how seriously He takes sin.
elements.questionHint3: The passage says God ’regretted’ making humankind and was ’highly offended’ — what do those emotional words tell you about whether God sees sin as a minor inconvenience or something that genuinely affects Him?

elements.passageText1: <sup>18</sup>Gama ana bayyana fushin Allah daga Sama, a kan dukan rashin bin Allah, da aikin mugunta na mutanen da suke danne gaskiya, ta aikin muguntarsu,
elements.passageText2: <sup>18</sup>Gama ana bayyana yadda Allah daga sama zai hukunta mutane cikin fushinsa a kan dukan rashin halin Allah da aikin mugunta na mutane waɗanda suke danne gaskiya ta wurin aikin muguntarsu.
elements.passageText5: <sup>18</sup>For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of people who suppress the truth by their unrighteousness,

elements.question3: What does Paul say is revealed from heaven? What does it mean to ’suppress the truth’?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul says that the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness. This is not a future threat only but something already being revealed — God’s settled, holy opposition to sin is an active reality in the world now. To ’suppress the truth’ means to hold down or restrain what one knows to be true — not ignorance, but deliberate resistance to truth that is available. Paul’s argument in the surrounding verses is that the existence and nature of God is visible in creation, so that people are without excuse. Suppressing the truth means choosing to push away what can be known about God in order to live without reference to Him.
elements.questionHint3: The word ’suppress’ implies that the truth is already present and known — you cannot suppress something you have never encountered. What does that tell you about Paul’s assumption regarding how much people already know about God before they are told the gospel?

elements.text3: In the world today, who is guilty of sinning?

elements.passageText1: <sup>9</sup>To, ƙaƙa? Mu Yahudawa mun fi sauran ne? A'a, ko kaɗan! Don dā ma mun ɗora wa Yahudawa da al'ummai laifi, cewa dukkansu zunubi yana iko da su. <sup>10</sup>3.10 Zab 14.1-3; 53.1-3 Kamar yadda yake a rubuce cewa, <sup>11</sup>Babu wani mai fahimta, babu wani mai neman Allah. <sup>12</sup>Duk sun bauɗe, sun zama marasa amfani baki ɗaya, ... <sup>23</sup>gama 'yan adam duka sun yi zunubi, sun kasa kuma ga ɗaukakar Allah.
elements.passageText2: <sup>9</sup>To, yaya ke nan? Ko mu Yahudawa mun fi sauran mutane ne? Ina! Ko kaɗan! Mun riga mun nuna cewa da Yahudawa da waɗanda ba Yahudawa ba duka suna da laifi, kuma duka suna ƙarƙashin ikon zunubi. <sup>10</sup>Kamar yadda yake a rubuce cikin Rubutacciyar Maganar Allah cewa, <sup>11</sup>Babu wani mai ganewa, <sup>12</sup>Duk sun juya wa Allah baya, ... <sup>23</sup>gama kowa ya yi zunubi, kuma kowa ya kāsa kai ga darajar Allah.
elements.passageText5: <sup>9</sup>What then? Are we better off? Certainly not, for we have already charged that Jews and Greeks alike are all under sin, <sup>10</sup>just as it is written: ‘There is no one righteous, not even one, <sup>11</sup>there is no one who understands, there is no one who seeks God. <sup>12</sup>All have turned away, together they have become worthless; there is no one who shows kindness, not even one.’ . . . <sup>23</sup>for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God,

elements.question3: Paul says there is no one who is righteous. How do you respond to that claim? Does it feel too sweeping?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul’s claim is deliberately absolute and universal — ‘not even one’ leaves no room for exceptions. Many people’s instinctive reaction is that this feels too sweeping: surely some people are genuinely good? But Paul is not saying no one ever does anything kind or admirable. He is saying that no one, measured against God’s standard, is righteous — no one fully seeks God, no one consistently does what is right in God’s eyes. The phrase ’fall short of the glory of God’ in verse 23 captures this: it is not that some people fall slightly short and others massively so — all of us fall short. The point is not to rank human behaviour but to establish that the problem of sin is universal, which is the necessary foundation for understanding why the gospel applies to everyone.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says ’not even one’ is righteous — he is not comparing people to each other but to God’s standard. How might the conclusion change if the measuring stick is God’s perfect character rather than a comparison with other human beings?

elements.text3: What is the consequence of our sin?

elements.passageText1: <sup>23</sup>Gama sakamakon zunubi mutuwa ne, amma baiwar Allah ita ce rai madawwami a cikin Almasihu Yesu Ubangijinmu.
elements.passageText2: <sup>23</sup>Gama sakamakon zunubi shi ne mutuwa, amma kyautar Allah ita ce rai na har abada ta wurin Almasihu Yesu Ubangijinmu.
elements.passageText5: <sup>23</sup>For the payoff of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.

elements.question3: What does Paul say sin earns? What contrast does he draw at the end of the verse?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul says the payoff — the earned wage — of sin is death. He uses the language of wages deliberately: death is not an arbitrary punishment but the just return for a life of sin, what sin deserves and earns. The contrast he draws at the end of the verse is striking and carefully constructed: against the wages of sin (something earned and deserved) he places the gift of God (something freely given and unearned). Death is what we earn; eternal life is what God gives. The two sides of the verse could not be more different — one is justice, the other is grace. And both are concentrated in the person of Jesus Christ, in whose name the gift is given.
elements.questionHint3: Paul deliberately uses the word ’payoff’ or ’wages’ for what sin produces — something that is earned — and then contrasts it with a ’gift’ for what God offers. Why is that distinction between earning and receiving as a gift so important to the point he is making?

elements.text3: What is the only hope for us?

elements.passageText5: <sup>21</sup>So I find this law at work: When I want to do good, evil is present with me. <sup>22</sup>For I delight in the law of God in my inner being. <sup>23</sup>But I see a different law in my members waging war against the law of my mind and making me captive to the law of sin that is in my members. <sup>24</sup>Wretched man that I am! Who will rescue me from this body of death? <sup>25</sup>Thanks be to God through Jesus Christ our Lord! So then, I myself serve the law of God with my mind, but with my flesh I serve the law of sin.

elements.question3: Paul describes an internal battle. Have you ever experienced what he describes — wanting to do good but finding yourself doing otherwise? Where does he say the rescue comes from?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul describes an experience many people recognise: the frustrating gap between knowing what is right and actually doing it, between intentions and actions. He does not describe a person indifferent to goodness — he describes someone who genuinely wants to do good and delights in God’s law, yet finds an opposing force within them winning the battle. His cry of ’wretched man that I am!’ is one of the most honest outbursts in the Bible. Crucially, he does not look to willpower, self-discipline, or moral effort for rescue — his question is ’who will rescue me?’, not ’what must I do?’ The answer is immediate: rescue comes through Jesus Christ. The solution to the internal battle is not trying harder but being rescued by another.
elements.questionHint3: Paul frames his question as ’who will rescue me?’ rather than ’what should I do to improve myself?’ — what does the choice of that word ’rescue’ tell you about whether Paul thinks the answer to sin lies within himself or outside himself?

elements.text3: The picture here is bleak — but it is honest. We cannot fix this ourselves. The good news is that God does not leave us without hope, which is exactly where the next study begins.
Key truths

Sin is rebellion against God. It came into the world through the first man and woman, Adam and Eve, as they deliberately disobeyed God.

"When the woman saw that the fruit of the tree was good for food and pleasing to the eye, and also desirable for gaining wisdom, she took some and ate it. She also gave some to her husband, who was with her, and he ate it." — Genesis 3:6

Every human being who has ever lived has sinned against God.

"...for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God..." — Romans 3:23
To think about

elements.question3: How would you explain to a friend the meaning of sin according to what the Bible teaches?
What do your friends think about sin? Do they think that they sin themselves?

elements.text3: In the last study we saw the seriousness of sin and its consequences. The picture was bleak. But God does not leave us there. This study is where the good news begins — we look at God’s extraordinary plan not to punish us, but to rescue us.
What does God think about His world?

elements.passageText1: <sup>16</sup>“Saboda ƙaunar da Allah ya yi wa duniya har ya ba da makaɗaicin Ɗansa, domin duk wanda ya gaskata da shi kada ya hallaka, sai dai ya sami rai madawwami.
elements.passageText2: <sup>16</sup>“Gama Allah ya ƙaunaci duniya sosai har ya ba da makaɗaicin Ɗansa, domin dukan wanda ya ba da gaskiya gare shi kada yă halaka, amma yă sami rai na har abada.
elements.passageText5: <sup>16</sup>For this is the way God loved the world: He gave his one and only Son, so that everyone who believes in him will not perish but have eternal life.

elements.question3: What does this verse tell us about God’s attitude towards the world, despite the sin we looked at in the last study?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Despite the world’s sin, God’s attitude is one of deep love — not anger or rejection. His love was so great that it moved him to action: he gave the most precious thing he had, his one and only Son. And the purpose of that gift was not punishment but rescue — so that anyone who believes would not perish but have eternal life. God’s response to human sinfulness is therefore not condemnation but an offer of salvation.
elements.questionHint3: What word does John use to describe God’s motivation, and what did that motivation lead God to do?

elements.text3: Why did God send His Son, Jesus, into the world?

elements.passageText1: <sup>45</sup>Domin Ɗan Mutum ma ya zo ba domin a bauta masa ba, sai dai domin shi ya yi bautar, ya kuma ba da ransa fansa saboda mutane da yawa.”
elements.passageText2: <sup>45</sup>Gama Ɗan Mutum ya zo ba domin a yi masa hidima ba, amma domin yă yi hidima, yă kuma ba da ransa domin ceton mutane da yawa.”
elements.passageText5: <sup>45</sup>For even the Son of Man did not come to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.

elements.question3: How does Jesus describe his own purpose? What does the word ’ransom’ imply about our situation?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus describes his purpose as coming not to be served but to serve, and specifically to give his life as a ransom for many. The word ’ransom’ implies that we are in a state of captivity or debt that we cannot pay ourselves — someone is held, and a price must be paid to secure their release. Applied to us, it means we are bound by sin and its penalty, and Jesus willingly gave his own life as the payment that sets us free.
elements.questionHint3: Think about what a ransom is used for in everyday life — what does that tell you about the condition of the people Jesus came to help?

elements.passageText1: <sup>8</sup>Amma kuwa Allah yana tabbatar mana da ƙaunar da yake yi mana, wato tun muna masu zunubi, Almasihu ya mutu dominmu.
elements.passageText2: <sup>8</sup>Amma Allah ya tabbatar mana yawan ƙaunarsa, da yake tun muna masu zunubi, Almasihu ya mutu saboda mu.
elements.passageText5: <sup>8</sup>But God demonstrates his own love for us, in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.

elements.question3: What makes God’s love here particularly striking, according to Paul?
elements.sampleAnswer3: What makes God’s love striking is the timing: Christ died for us while we were still sinners. We had not cleaned up our act, turned to God, or earned his favour in any way. Normally we might expect someone to sacrifice themselves for a good or deserving person — but God demonstrated his love at the very point when we were least deserving of it. The love is unconditional and entirely one-directional; it comes from God’s character, not from anything in us.
elements.questionHint3: Notice the word ’while’ — at what point in our moral condition did Christ die, and why does the timing matter?

elements.passageText1: <sup>15</sup>Maganar nan tabbatacciya ce, ta kuma cancanci a karɓe ta ɗungum cewa, “Almasihu Yesu ya shigo duniya ne domin ceton masu zunubi,” ni ne kuwa babbansu.
elements.passageText2: <sup>15</sup>Maganar nan tabbatacciya ce, kuma ta isa a karɓe ta gaba ɗaya cewa, “Almasihu Yesu ya zo duniya domin ceton masu zunubi,” a cikinsu duka ni ne mafi zunubi.
elements.passageText5: <sup>15</sup>This saying is trustworthy and deserves full acceptance: ‘Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners’—and I am the worst of them!

elements.question3: Paul calls himself ’the worst’ of sinners. Why do you think he says this? What does it tell us about who Jesus came to save?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul is probably referring to his past as a violent persecutor of Christians — someone who had actively hunted down and imprisoned followers of Jesus. He does not say this to be falsely modest, but to make a point: if Christ came to save even him, then no one is beyond reach. It tells us that Jesus did not come only for the morally respectable or the mildly flawed — he came to save sinners, full stop. The scope of salvation is as wide as the depth of human sinfulness.
elements.questionHint3: If even the ’worst’ sinner is included in Christ’s saving purpose, what does that imply about whether anyone could be considered too sinful to be saved?

elements.text3: What is the result of Jesus’ death?

elements.passageText1: <sup>9</sup>To, tun da yake a yanzu an kuɓutar da mu ta wurin jininsa, ashe kuwa, ta wurinsa za mu fi samun kārewa daga fushin Allah ke nan.
elements.passageText2: <sup>9</sup>To, tun da yake mun sami zaman marasa laifi a gaban Allah ta wurin jinin Almasihu, balle fa yanzu, ai, za a cece mu daga fushin Allah ta wurin Almasihu.
elements.passageText5: <sup>9</sup>Much more then, because we have now been declared righteous by his blood, we will be saved through him from God’s wrath.

elements.question3: According to Romans 5:9, what are those who trust in Christ declared to be, and what are they saved from?
elements.sampleAnswer3: According to Romans 5:9, those who trust in Christ are declared righteous — a legal verdict made possible by Jesus’ blood (his atoning death). And as a result of that declaration, they are saved from God’s wrath. This is significant: God’s wrath is his righteous judgment against sin, which we all deserve. But through Christ, that judgment has been dealt with, and believers are no longer under its threat.
elements.questionHint3: Paul uses a courtroom-style word — what verdict is pronounced over believers, and what danger does that verdict protect them from?

elements.passageText1: <sup>16</sup>“Saboda ƙaunar da Allah ya yi wa duniya har ya ba da makaɗaicin Ɗansa, domin duk wanda ya gaskata da shi kada ya hallaka, sai dai ya sami rai madawwami. <sup>17</sup>Gama Allah bai aiko Ɗansa duniya domin yă yanke mata hukunci ba, sai dai domin duniya ta sami ceto ta wurinsa.
elements.passageText2: <sup>16</sup>“Gama Allah ya ƙaunaci duniya sosai har ya ba da makaɗaicin Ɗansa, domin dukan wanda ya ba da gaskiya gare shi kada yă halaka, amma yă sami rai na har abada. <sup>17</sup>Gama Allah bai aiko Ɗansa duniya domin yă hukunta duniya ba, amma domin duniya ta sami ceto ta wurinsa.
elements.passageText5: <sup>16</sup>For this is the way God loved the world: He gave his one and only Son, so that everyone who believes in him will not perish but have eternal life. <sup>17</sup>For God did not send his Son into the world to condemn the world, but that the world should be saved through him.

elements.question3: What does Jesus promise to all who believe in him, according to John 3:16-17?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus promises that everyone who believes in him will not perish but will have eternal life. Verse 17 adds to this by clarifying God’s intention: he did not send his Son to condemn but to save. So believers gain both a positive gift — eternal life — and an escape from a negative outcome — condemnation and perishing. The promise is open to everyone (‘everyone who believes’), and it comes directly through faith in God’s Son.
elements.questionHint3: Jesus gives two outcomes for those who believe — one thing they escape and one thing they receive. Can you identify both?

elements.text3: What does the Bible say about those who do not believe in Jesus?

elements.passageText1: <sup>18</sup>Duk wanda ya gaskata da shi, ba za a yi masa hukunci ba. Wanda kuwa bai ba da gaskiya ba, an riga an yi masa hukunci, domin bai gaskata da sunan makaɗaicin Ɗan Allah ba.
elements.passageText2: <sup>18</sup>“Duk wanda ya ba da gaskiya ga Ɗan Allah, ba za a hukunta shi ba. Amma duk wanda bai ba da gaskiya gare shi ba, an riga an hukunta shi ke nan domin bai ba da gaskiya ga sunan makaɗaicin Ɗan Allah ba.
elements.passageText5: <sup>18</sup>The one who believes in him is not condemned. The one who does not believe has been condemned already, because he has not believed in the name of the one and only Son of God.

elements.question3: Jesus draws a sharp distinction here. What is the difference between the situation of the one who believes and the one who does not?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The believer is not condemned — the verdict against them has been lifted through faith in Jesus. The non-believer, however, stands condemned already — not as a future possibility but as a present reality. Crucially, the condemnation is not because of a list of specific sins but because of a single issue: not believing in the name of God’s one and only Son. Jesus presents these as two completely different standing before God, with belief in him as the decisive factor that separates them.
elements.questionHint3: Notice that John says the unbeliever ’has been condemned already’ — what does the tense of that phrase suggest about when this judgment takes effect?

elements.passageText1: <sup>5</sup>Wannan kuwa ita ce tabbatar hukunci mai adalci na Allah, domin a lasafta ku a cikin waɗanda suka cancanci zama a Mulkin Allah, wanda kuke shan wuya dominsa, <sup>6</sup>domin kuwa Allah ya ga adalci ne, yă yi sakamokon ƙunci ga waɗanda suke ƙuntata muku, <sup>7</sup>yă kuma ba ku hutu har da mu ma, ku da ake ƙuntata wa ɗin, a ranar bayyanar Ubangiji Yesu daga Sama tare da manyan mala'ikunsa, a cikin wuta mai ruruwa, <sup>8</sup>yana ta sāka wa waɗanda suke ƙi sanin Allah, da kuma waɗanda suka ƙi bin bisharar Ubangijinmu Yesu. <sup>9</sup>1.9 Ish 2.10 Za su sha hukuncin madawwamiyar hallaka, suna a ware da zatin Ubangiji, da kuma, maɗaukakin ikonsa, <sup>10</sup>wato, a lokacin da ya dawo, tsarkakansa su ɗaukaka shi, duk masu ba da gaskiya kuma su yi al'ajabinsa a ranar nan, don kun gaskata shaidar da muka yi muku.
elements.passageText2: <sup>5</sup>Dukan wannan tabbaci cewa shariˈar Allah mai adalci ce, da nufi cewa a mai da ku yadda za ku cancanci zama a cikin mulkin Allah, gama a kan mulkin nan ne kuke shan wahala. <sup>6</sup>Allah mai adalci ne, saboda haka su waɗanda suke ba ku wahala zai sāka musu da wahala, <sup>7</sup>amma ku da kuke shan wahala kuwa zai hutar da ku, har ma da mu. Wannan zai faru a ranar da Ubangiji Yesu ya bayyana daga sama tare da manyan malaˈikunsa a cikin harsunan wuta. <sup>8</sup>Zai kuwa hukunta waɗanda suka ƙi sanin Allah, da kuma waɗanda suka ƙi biyayya da labari mai daɗi na Ubangijinmu Yesu. <sup>9</sup>Za su sha hukuncin halaka ta har abada, za a kawar da su daga gaban Ubangiji da kuma ɗaukakar ikonsa. 1.9 Ish 2.10 <sup>10</sup>A Ranar nan zai dawo domin tsarkakansa su ɗaukaka shi, dukan masu ba da gaskiya kuma su yi mamakinsa. Wannan ma ya haɗa duk da ku, gama kun gaskata da shaidarmu.
elements.passageText5: <sup>5</sup>This is evidence of God’s righteous judgment, to make you worthy of the kingdom of God, for which you are suffering. <sup>6</sup>For it is right for God to repay with affliction those who afflict you, <sup>7</sup>and to give relief to you who are afflicted and to us as well, when the Lord Jesus is revealed from heaven with his mighty angels. <sup>8</sup>With flaming fire he will mete out punishment on those who do not know God and do not obey the gospel of our Lord Jesus. <sup>9</sup>They will undergo the penalty of eternal destruction, away from the presence of the Lord and from the glory of his strength, <sup>10</sup>when he comes to be glorified among his saints and admired on that day among all who have believed—and you did believe our testimony to you.

elements.question3: How does Paul describe the fate of those who do not know God and do not obey the gospel? How does this sit with you?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul describes their fate as ’eternal destruction, away from the presence of the Lord and from the glory of his strength.’ This is not annihilation but permanent separation from God — the source of all goodness, light, and life. It is a sobering description, and many people find it deeply uncomfortable. It raises hard questions about fairness and the character of God. Yet Paul frames it within God’s righteous justice: it is a right response to those who have rejected both God and the gospel. How it sits with us personally will vary, and that honest reaction is worth exploring.
elements.questionHint3: Paul uses a specific phrase to describe what this destruction means relationally — what are those people separated from, and why might that detail matter more than the word ’destruction’ alone?

elements.text3: How does Jesus’ death save us?

elements.passageText1: <sup>46</sup>27.46 Zab 22.1 Wajen ƙarfe uku kuma sai Yesu ya ɗaga murya da ƙarfi ya ce, “Eloi, Eloi, lama sabaktāni?” Wato, “Ya Allahna, ya Allahna, don me ka yashe ni?”
elements.passageText2: <sup>46</sup>Wajen ƙarfe uku kuma Yesu ya ɗaga murya da ƙarfi ya ce, “Eloi, Eloi, lama sabaktani?” wato, “Ya Allahna, ya Allahna, don me ka yashe ni?” 27.46 Zab 22.1
elements.passageText5: 46At about three o’clock Jesus shouted with a loud voice, ‘Eli, Eli, lema sabachthani?’ (that is, ‘My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?’).

elements.question3: What does Jesus cry out from the cross? What might it mean that the Son of God was ’forsaken’ by his Father at this moment?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus cries out ’My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?’ — a cry of real abandonment, not just distress. For the eternal Son who had always existed in perfect unity with the Father, this forsakenness represents something almost unthinkable: a rupture in that relationship. Theologically, it points to Jesus bearing the full weight of sin’s consequence — separation from God — in our place. The judgment that should fall on us for our sin fell on him instead, which is why he experienced what we deserved.
elements.questionHint3: Given what we have already seen about the consequence of sin (separation from God), what might it mean that Jesus experienced that same separation on the cross?

elements.passageText1: <sup>21</sup>Shi da bai san zunubi ba, Allah ya mai da shi zunubi saboda mu, domin mu mu sami adalcin Allah ta wurinsa.
elements.passageText2: <sup>21</sup>Almasihu bai taɓa yin zunubi ba, amma saboda mu Allah ya mai da shi kamar mai zunubi. Ta wannan dalilin ne, cikin Almasihu, za mu iya sami adalcin Allah.
elements.passageText5: <sup>21</sup>God made the one who did not know sin to be sin for us, so that in him we would become the righteousness of God.

elements.question3: What exchange does Paul describe in this verse? What did Jesus take on, and what do believers receive?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul describes a remarkable double exchange. Jesus, who had no sin of his own (‘the one who did not know sin’), was made to be sin for us — he took on our sin and its full consequence. In return, believers receive the righteousness of God — not a righteousness they earned, but one that belongs to God himself, credited to them through union with Christ. It is a swap of opposites: our sin goes to him; his righteousness comes to us.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says Jesus ’did not know sin’ — so what makes the exchange he describes so surprising, and what does each party in the exchange give and receive?

elements.passageText1: <sup>13</sup>3.13 M.Sh 21.23 Almasihu ya fanso mu daga la'anar nan ta Shari'a, da ya zama abin la'ana saboda mu, domin a rubuce yake cewa, “Duk wanda aka kafa a jikin itace, la'ananne ne.”
elements.passageText2: <sup>13</sup>Laˈanar nan ta Koyarwar Musa mun kuɓuta daga gare ta, saboda Almasihu ya fanshe mu daga laˈanar nan, da yake an mai da shi abin laˈana saboda mu ne. Gama an rubuta, “Duk wanda aka rataye shi a kan gungumen itace, laˈananne ne ga Allah.” 3.13 M. Sha 21.23
elements.passageText5: <sup>13</sup>Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us (for it is written, ‘Cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree’);

elements.question3: What ’curse’ does Paul say Christ took on? What does it mean that he ’redeemed’ us from it?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The curse Paul refers to is the curse of the law — the judgment and condemnation that falls on everyone who fails to keep God’s law perfectly (which is all of us). Christ took that curse on himself by hanging on a cross, which the Old Testament specifically associated with being cursed by God. To ’redeem’ us means to buy us out of that condition — like purchasing the freedom of a slave. Christ paid the price of the curse so that we would no longer be under it.
elements.questionHint3: What does it mean to ’redeem’ something in everyday life (think of redeeming a voucher or buying something back), and how does that shed light on what Christ did for us?

elements.passageText1: <sup>24</sup>2.24 Ish 53.5,6 Shi kansa ya ɗauke zunubanmu a jikinsa a kan gungume, domin mu yi zamanmu matattu ga zunubi, rayayyu kuma ga adalci. Da raunukansa ne aka warkar da ku.
elements.passageText2: <sup>24</sup>Mun yi zunubi, amma shi ya ɗauki zunubanmu a jikinsa saˈad da aka gicciye shi a kan gungumen itace. Ya yi haka domin ta wurin mutuwarsa mu ma mu mutu ga zunubi, mu rayu ga yin adalci. Kamar yadda aka rubuta, “Ta wurin raunin da aka yi masa ne kun sami warkewa.” 2.24 Ish 53.5
elements.passageText5: <sup>24</sup>He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree, that we may cease from sinning and live for righteousness. By his wounds you were healed.

elements.question3: What did Jesus bear on the cross according to Peter, and what does Peter say the purpose of this was for us?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Peter says Jesus bore our sins in his body on the cross — he physically and personally carried them. The purpose was twofold: that we might cease from sinning (freedom from sin’s power) and live for righteousness (a new positive direction of life). Peter also adds that by Jesus’ wounds we were healed — using the language of healing to describe what the cross accomplishes spiritually. The cross is not only about forgiveness of past sin but about transformation for the future.
elements.questionHint3: Peter mentions two results of Jesus bearing our sins — one is about stopping something and the other is about starting something. Can you identify what each is?

elements.text3: The verses above describe what Jesus was doing on the cross. He was not simply dying as a martyr or a good example — He was taking the penalty for our sin in our place. This is what makes His death different from any other death in history.
Why was Jesus not defeated by death?

elements.passageText1: <sup>24</sup>2.24 Mat 28.5,6; Mar 16.6; Luk 24.5 Amma Allah ya tashe shi bayan ya ɓalle masa ƙangin mutuwa, domin ba shi yiwuwa mutuwa ta riƙe shi.
elements.passageText2: <sup>24</sup>Amma Allah ya fanshe shi daga ikon mutuwa, ya tashe shi daga matattu da yake ba ya yiwuwa mutuwa ta riƙe shi. 2.24 Mat 28.5-6; Mar 16.6; Luk 24.5
elements.passageText5: <sup>24</sup>But God raised him up, having released him from the pains of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it.

elements.question3: What did God do, and why does Peter say death could not hold Jesus?
elements.sampleAnswer3: God raised Jesus up, releasing him from death. Peter’s reason is striking: it was simply not possible for death to hold him. Death has no ultimate claim over the one who is the source of life itself and who had no sin of his own. The resurrection is therefore not a surprise reversal but an inevitability — death had no rightful power over Jesus once the work of bearing sin was complete.
elements.questionHint3: Peter says it was ’not possible’ for death to hold Jesus — what does that strong word suggest about the relationship between Jesus and death?

elements.text3: How is Jesus described by Paul?

elements.passageText1: <sup>5</sup>Domin Allah ɗaya ne, matsakanci kuma ɗaya ne, a tsakanin Allah da mutane, Almasihu Yesu, mutum, <sup>6</sup>wanda ya ba da kansa fansa saboda kowa da kowa. An kuwa yi shaidar wannan a daidai lokacinsa.
elements.passageText2: <sup>5</sup>Gama Allah ɗaya ne, <sup>6</sup>Shi ne ya ba da kansa
elements.passageText5: <sup>5</sup>For there is one God and one intermediary between God and humanity, Christ Jesus, himself human, <sup>6</sup>who gave himself as a ransom for all, revealing God’s purpose at his appointed time.

elements.question3: What two things does Paul say are uniquely ’one’ according to this passage? What role does Jesus fulfil between God and humanity?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul says there is one God and one intermediary between God and humanity. The two ’ones’ are therefore God himself and the mediator — Christ Jesus. Jesus fulfils the role of intermediary or mediator: the one who stands between two parties and brings them together. He is uniquely qualified for this because he is himself human, yet also gave himself as a ransom for all. No one else bridges that gap — not religious leaders, not our own good works, only Christ.
elements.questionHint3: An intermediary or mediator stands between two parties — who are the two parties here, and what does Jesus do to bring them together?

elements.question: What do you think of when you hear the word salvation?
elements.answer: ’Salvation’ can sound like old-fashioned religious language, but it simply means rescue. The Bible uses it to describe what God has done for us through Jesus — rescuing us from the consequences of sin (God’s judgement) and restoring us to a right relationship with Him. Salvation is not something we achieve through being good enough; it is something God has done and freely offers to us. Receiving it requires a response, which is what Study 8 explores.

elements.text3: Key truths

Jesus is the only way to God.

‘Jesus answered, ‘I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me...’ — John 14:6

All who believe in God’s Son will have eternal life: all who do not believe will be condemned.

‘For God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son, that whoever believes in him shall not perish but have eternal life. Whoever believes in him is not condemned, but whoever does not believe stands condemned already because they have not believed in the name of God’s one and only Son.’ — John 3:16, 18
To think about

elements.question3: If your friends feel certain that God will accept them because they have always been honest and helped others, what would you say to them?
How would you answer a friend who argues that a God of love would not inflict everlasting punishment on anyone (except, perhaps, the very worst criminals)?

chapters.chapterTitle3: God’s Remedy: 2 The Resurrection and Ascension

elements.text3: The cross is not the end of the story. God the Father raised Jesus from the dead — and that changes everything. Christians do not worship a dead teacher; they worship a risen Saviour and living Lord. In this study we explore what the resurrection and ascension mean for us.
Why is the raising of Jesus from the dead (His resurrection) so important?

elements.passageText1: <sup>14</sup>In kuwa ba a ta da Almasihu ba, to, ashe, wa'azinmu a banza ne, bangaskiyarku kuma banza ce. ... <sup>17</sup>In kuwa ba a ta da Almasihu ba, ashe, bangaskiyarku banza ce, har yanzu kuma kuna tare da zunubanku. <sup>18</sup>Ashe, waɗanda suka yi barci a kan su na Almasihu, sun hallaka ke nan. <sup>19</sup>Da begenmu ga Almasihu domin wannan rai ne kaɗai, ai, da mun fi kowa zama abin tausayi.
elements.passageText2: <sup>14</sup>Idan kuwa Almasihu bai tashi daga matattu ba, ashe, waˈazinmu banza ne, bangaskiyarku kuma ta zama banza. ... <sup>17</sup>Idan kuwa ba a tā da Almasihu ba, ashe, bangaskiyarku banza ce, har yanzu kuma kuna cikin zunubanku. <sup>18</sup>Ashe, waɗanda suka mutu cikin Almasihu, sun halaka ke nan. <sup>19</sup>Idan sa zuciyarmu ga Almasihu domin rayuwar nan ne kaɗai take, ai, mun fi kowa zama abin tausayi ke nan.
elements.passageText5: <sup>14</sup>And if Christ has not been raised, then our preaching is futile and your faith is empty. . . <sup>17</sup>And if Christ has not been raised, your faith is useless; you are still in your sins. <sup>18</sup>Furthermore, those who have fallen asleep in Christ have also perished. <sup>19</sup>For if only in this life we have hope in Christ, we should be pitied more than anyone.

elements.question3: Paul lists several things that would be true if Christ had not been raised. What are they? What does this tell you about how central the resurrection is to the Christian faith?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul lists four devastating consequences that would follow if Christ had not been raised: Christian preaching would be futile and empty; faith would be useless; believers would still be in their sins — unforgiven; and those who have already died as Christians would have simply perished. He adds a fifth: if our hope in Christ extends only to this life, Christians are to be pitied more than anyone, having given up much for nothing. Taken together, these show that the resurrection is not a peripheral detail but the very foundation of the Christian faith. Without it, everything else — forgiveness, eternal life, future hope — collapses.
elements.questionHint3: Paul uses the word ’if’ repeatedly — try listing each consequence he names one by one, and then ask yourself what they have in common.

elements.text3: What proof is there that Jesus rose from the dead?

Mutanen suka ce musu, “Don me kuke neman rayayye a cikin matattu? <sup>6</sup>24.6 Mat 16.21; 17.22,23; 20.18,19; Mar 8.31; 9.31; 10.33,34; Luk 9.22; 18.31-33 Ai, ba ya nan, ya tashi. Ku tuna yadda ya gaya muku tun yana Galili <sup>7</sup>cewa, ‘Lalle ne a ba da Ɗan Mutum ga mutane masu zunubi, a gicciye shi, a rana ta uku kuma ya tashi’.”
