Ku tuna da abin da ya gaya muku tun yana Galili. <sup>7</sup>Ya ce, ‘Lallai ne a ba da Ɗan Mutum a hannun mutane masu zunubi, a gicciye shi, a rana ta uku kuma yă tashi.’ ” 24.6-7 Mat 16.21; 17.22-23; 20.18-19; Mar 8.31; 9.31; 10.33-34; Luk 9.22; 18.31-33
elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>Now on the first day of the week, at early dawn, the women went to the tomb, taking the aromatic spices they had prepared. <sup>2</sup>They found that the stone had been rolled away from the tomb, <sup>3</sup>but when they went in, they did not find the body of the Lord Jesus. <sup>4</sup>While they were perplexed about this, suddenly two men stood beside them in gleaming clothing. <sup>5</sup>The women were terribly frightened and bowed their faces to the ground, but the men said to them, ‘Why do you look for the living among the dead? <sup>6</sup>He is not here, but has been raised! Remember how he told you, while he was still in Galilee, <sup>7</sup>that the Son of Man must be delivered into the hands of sinful men, and be crucified, and on the third day rise again.’

elements.question3: What did the women find when they arrived at the tomb? What do the two men tell them, and what had Jesus himself predicted?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The women arrived expecting to anoint a body, but found the stone already rolled away and the tomb empty — Jesus’ body was gone. Two men in gleaming clothing appeared and told them plainly: ‘He is not here, but has been raised.’ They then reminded the women of something Jesus himself had predicted while still in Galilee — that the Son of Man would be handed over, crucified, and would rise again on the third day. The empty tomb was therefore not a mystery to be explained away; it was the fulfilment of Jesus’ own prior announcement.
elements.questionHint3: The two men ask the women a pointed question — what does the question ’Why do you look for the living among the dead?’ imply about who they believe Jesus to be?

elements.passageText1: <sup>36</sup>Suna cikin faɗar waɗannan abubuwa, sai ga Yesu da kansa tsaye a tsakiyarsu, ya ce musu, “Salama alaikun!” <sup>37</sup>Amma suka firgita, tsoro ya kama su, suka zaci fatalwa suke gani. <sup>38</sup>Sai ya ce musu, “Don me kuka firgita, kuke kuma tantama a zuciyarku? <sup>39</sup>Ku dubi hannuwana da ƙafafuna, ai, ni ne da kaina. Ku taɓa ni, ku ji, don fatalwa ba ta da nama da ƙashi, yadda kuke gani nake da su.” <sup>40</sup>Da ya faɗi haka, ya nuna musu hannuwansa da ƙafafunsa. <sup>41</sup>Amma tun suna da sauran shakka saboda tsananin farin ciki da mamaki, sai ya ce musu, “Kuna da wani abinci a nan?” <sup>42</sup>Sai suka ba shi wata tsokar gasasshen kifi. <sup>43</sup>Ya kuwa karɓa, ya ci a gabansu.
elements.passageText2: <sup>36</sup>Tun suna magana game da wannan, sai kawai ga Yesu da kansa a tsaye a tsakiyarsu, ya ce musu, “Salama a gare ku!” 24.36 ya ce musu, “Salama a gare ku.”: Waɗansu kofe na rubuce-rubucen hannu na dā ba su da waɗannan kalmomin. <sup>37</sup>Tsoro ya kama su suna rawar jiki, suna tsammani fatalwa ce suke gani. <sup>38</sup>Sai ya ce musu, “Don me kuka ji tsoro haka, kuke kuma shakka a zuciyarku? <sup>39</sup>Ku duba hannuwana da ƙafafuna ku gani, ai, ni ne da kaina. Ku taɓa ni, ku ji, don fatalwa ba ta da nama da ƙashi, yadda kuke gani nake da su.” <sup>40</sup>Da ya faɗa haka, sai ya nuna musu hannuwansa da ƙafafunsa. 24.40 Waɗansu kofe na rubuce-rubucen hannu na dā ba su da aya ta 40. <sup>41</sup>Tun suna da sauran shakka saboda yawan farin ciki da mamaki, sai ya ce musu, “Kuna da abinci a nan kuwa?” <sup>42</sup>Suka ba shi tsokar gasasshen kifi. <sup>43</sup>Ya karɓa ya ci, su kuma suna kallonsa.
elements.passageText5: <sup>36</sup>While they were saying these things, Jesus himself stood among them and said to them, ‘Peace be with you.’ <sup>37</sup>But they were startled and terrified, thinking they saw a spirit. <sup>38</sup>Then he said to them, ‘Why are you frightened, and why do doubts arise in your hearts? <sup>39</sup>Look at my hands and my feet; it’s me myself! Touch me and see; a spirit does not have flesh and bones like you see I have.’ <sup>40</sup>And when he had said this, he showed them his hands and his feet. <sup>41</sup>Now while they still could not believe it because of their joy and were amazing themselves, he said to them, ‘Do you have anything here to eat?’ <sup>42</sup>So they gave him a piece of broiled fish, <sup>43</sup>and he took it and ate it in their presence.

elements.question3: How does Jesus demonstrate to the disciples that he is not a ghost or a vision? What details in this account suggest the resurrection was physical and bodily?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus addresses their assumption that he is a spirit directly: he invites them to look at and touch his hands and feet, and explicitly states that ’a spirit does not have flesh and bones like you see I have.’ He shows them his physical wounds. Then, to remove any remaining doubt, he asks for food and eats a piece of broiled fish in front of them. Ghosts do not eat. Each detail — the tangible body, the visible wounds, the capacity to consume food — underlines that this was a real, bodily resurrection, not a vision, hallucination, or spiritual apparition.
elements.questionHint3: Jesus gives the disciples three different ways to verify that he is physically present — can you identify each one, and why might each type of evidence matter?

elements.passageText1: <sup>19</sup>A ran nan da magariba, ranar farko ta mako, ƙofofin ɗakin da almajiran suke ciki na kulle don tsoron Yahudawa, sai ga Yesu ya zo ya tsaya a tsaka, ya ce musu, “Salama alaikun!” <sup>20</sup>Da ya faɗi haka, ya nuna musu hannuwansa da kwiɓinsa. Sai almajiran suka yi farin ciki da ganin Ubangiji.
elements.passageText2: <sup>19</sup>Da yamma ta yi a ranar farko ta mako, almajiran Yesu suna wuri ɗaya. Sun kuma kukkulle ƙofofi domin suna tsoron shugabannin Yahudawa. Sai ga Yesu ya zo ya tsaya a tsakiyarsu, ya ce musu, “Salama ta kasance tare da ku.” <sup>20</sup>Bayan da ya faɗa wannan magana, ya nuna musu hannuwansa da wajen haƙarƙarinsa. Sai almajiransa suka yi farin ciki da ganin Ubangiji.
elements.passageText5: <sup>19</sup>On the evening of that day, the first day of the week, the disciples had gathered together and locked the doors of the place because they were afraid of the Jewish leaders. Jesus came and stood among them and said to them, ‘Peace be with you.’ <sup>20</sup>When he had said this, he showed them his hands and his side. Then the disciples rejoiced when they saw the Lord.

elements.question3: The disciples were hiding behind locked doors in fear. How did their emotional state change when Jesus appeared? What significance do you see in him showing them his wounds?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The transformation is striking: they went from fear — hiding behind locked doors, afraid of arrest — to rejoicing, simply because they saw the Lord. The change was immediate and total. As for the wounds: Jesus did not appear in a glorified body with no trace of the cross. He showed them his hands and his side — the marks of the nails and the spear. This confirms his identity beyond doubt (this is the same Jesus who was crucified), and it shows that the resurrection did not erase what he suffered. His wounds are permanent marks of his sacrifice.
elements.questionHint3: Think about why Jesus specifically showed his wounds rather than simply speaking to them — what would the wounds prove that words alone might not?

M. 9.3-6
elements.passageText5: <sup>5</sup>and that he appeared to Cephas, then to the twelve. <sup>6</sup>Then he appeared to more than five hundred of the brothers and sisters at one time, most of whom are still alive, though some have fallen asleep. <sup>7</sup>Then he appeared to James, then to all the apostles. <sup>8</sup>Last of all, as though to one born at the wrong time, he appeared also to me.

elements.question3: Paul lists a range of witnesses to the risen Jesus. What is significant about the fact that he mentions over five hundred people at one time, ‘most of whom are still alive’?
elements.sampleAnswer3: By noting that most of the five hundred are still alive, Paul is effectively issuing an open invitation to verify the claim. His readers could, in principle, go and ask these witnesses themselves. A fabricated story would not survive that kind of scrutiny — you do not cite five hundred living eyewitnesses to an event that did not happen. The sheer number also rules out the possibility of a private hallucination shared by just one or two people; a crowd of over five hundred experiencing the same thing simultaneously is a different category of evidence entirely.
elements.questionHint3: Why might Paul have specifically pointed out that most of the five hundred are ’still alive’ — what would his original readers have been able to do with that information?

elements.text3: What was achieved by the resurrection?

elements.passageText1: <sup>54</sup>15.54 Ish 25.8 Sa'ad da kuma marar ruɓa ya maye mai ruɓar nan, marar mutuwa kuma ya maye mai mutuwar nan, a sa'an nan ne fa maganar nan da take a rubuce za a cika ta, cewa, <sup>55</sup>15.55 Yush 13.14 “Ke mutuwa, ina nasararki? <sup>56</sup>Ai, zunubi shi ne ƙarin mutuwa, Shari'a kuwa ita ce sanadin ƙarfin zunubi.
elements.passageText2: <sup>54</sup>Saˈad da kuma jiki mai ruɓan nan ya canja ya zama marar ruɓa, kuma jiki mai mutuwar nan ya zama marar mutuwa, a lokacin nan ne fa maganar nan da take a rubuce cikin Maganar Allah za ta cika, cewa, <sup>55</sup>Ke, mutuwa, ina nasararki? <sup>56</sup>Ai, zunubi shi ne dafin mutuwa, zunubi kuwa ya sami ikonsa ta dalilin Koyarwar.
elements.passageText5: <sup>54</sup>Now when this perishable puts on the imperishable, and this mortal puts on immortality, then the saying that is written will happen: ‘Death has been swallowed up in victory.’ <sup>55</sup>’Where, O death, is your victory? Where, O death, is your sting?’ <sup>56</sup>The sting of death is sin, and the power of sin is the law.

elements.question3: How does Paul describe what the resurrection has done to death? What is the ’sting’ of death, and why has it lost its power?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul describes death as having been ’swallowed up in victory’ — completely defeated and absorbed. He taunts death as though it were a beaten opponent: ‘Where is your victory? Where is your sting?’ The sting of death, Paul explains, is sin — sin is what gives death its lethal power, because sin incurs judgment. But Christ has dealt with sin on the cross. With sin dealt with and the resurrection accomplished, death has lost its sting. It can still happen physically, but it no longer has the final word or the power to condemn.
elements.questionHint3: Paul tells us directly what the ’sting’ of death is — once you identify it, ask yourself what in this study has already dealt with that very thing.

elements.passageText1: <sup>11</sup>8.11 1Kor 3.16 In kuwa Ruhun wannan da ya ta da Yesu daga matattu yana zaune a zuciyarku, to, shi da ya ta da Almasihu Yesu daga matattu zai kuma raya jikin nan naku mai mutuwa, ta wurin Ruhunsa da yake a zaune a zuciyarku.
elements.passageText2: <sup>11</sup>Idan kuma Ruhun Allah, wanda ya tā da Yesu daga matattu yana zama a zukatanku, to, shi wanda ya tā da Almasihu daga matattu zai ba da rai ga jikinku mai mutuwa ta wurin Ruhunsa wanda yake zama a zukatanku. 8.11 1 Kor 3.16
elements.passageText5: <sup>11</sup>Moreover if the Spirit of the one who raised Jesus from the dead lives in you, the one who raised Christ from the dead will also make your mortal bodies alive through his Spirit who lives in you.

elements.question3: What promise does Paul make to those in whom the Spirit lives? What connection does he draw between Jesus’ resurrection and ours?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul promises that the same God who raised Jesus from the dead will also make believers’ mortal bodies alive — through his Spirit who lives in them. The connection he draws is direct: it is the same power, the same agent (the Spirit), and the same God at work. Jesus’ resurrection is therefore not a one-off event for him alone; it is the pattern and guarantee of what awaits all who have the Spirit. What happened to Jesus will happen to his people.
elements.questionHint3: Paul describes the Spirit using a phrase that points back to the resurrection of Jesus — how does that description link what happened to Jesus with what will happen to believers?

elements.text3: What does the resurrection teach us about God?

elements.passageText1: <sup>19</sup>da kuma ko mene ne girman ikonsa marar misaltuwa da yake zartarwa a gare mu, mu masu ba da gaskiya, wato zartarwar ƙarfin ikon nan nasa, <sup>20</sup>1.20 Zab 110.1 wanda ya zartar ga Almasihu sa'ad da ya tashe shi daga matattu, ya kuma ba shi wurin zama a damansa a samaniya,
elements.passageText2: <sup>19</sup>Ina adduˈa har yanzu ku san ko mene ne girman ikonsa wanda ya wuce gaban misali, wanda yake aiki a cikinmu, mu da muka ba da gaskiya. Ikon nan da yake aiki a cikinmu, ɗaya ne da ikon nan <sup>20</sup>wanda ya yi aiki cikin Almasihu saˈad da ya tashe shi daga matattu, ya kuma ba shi wurin zama mai daraja a hannun damansa a sama. 1.20 Zab 110.1
elements.passageText5: <sup>19</sup>and what is the incomparable greatness of his power toward us who believe, as displayed in the exercise of his immense strength. <sup>20</sup>This power he exercised in Christ when he raised him from the dead and seated him at his right hand in the heavenly realms,

elements.question3: Paul says the resurrection displays God’s ’incomparable greatness’ of power. What does that mean for believers? How does knowing this affect the way you think about God?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The word ’incomparable’ means there is nothing to measure it against — no greater power exists. And Paul says this same power is directed toward believers. The resurrection is therefore not just a demonstration of what God can do in general; it is a display of the power that is actively at work for those who believe. For how we think about God, this is significant: the God we are relating to is not limited, distant, or passive. He is the God who raised the dead, and that same strength is on our side.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says this power is directed ’toward us who believe’ — so what does the resurrection tell us not just about God’s ability, but about his posture towards his people?

elements.text3: How did Jesus ascend into heaven?

Yesun nan da aka ɗauke daga gare ku aka kai shi Sama, zai komo ne ta yadda kuka ga tafiyarsa zuwa Sama.”
elements.passageText2: <sup>9</sup>Da ya faɗa haka, suna cikin dubawa, sai aka ɗauke shi zuwa sama. Wani girgije ya rufe shi, ba su ƙara ganinsa ba. 1.9 Mar 16.19; Luk 24.50-51 <sup>10</sup>Saˈad da suke zuba ido sama, shi kuma yana tafiya, sai ga mutum biyu a tsaye kusa da su, sanye da fararen riguna. <sup>11</sup>Sai suka ce, “Ku mutanen Galili, don me kuke tsaye kuna kallon sama? Yesun nan da aka ɗauke daga wurinku zuwa sama, zai sāke dawowa kamar yadda kuka ga ya tafi zuwa sama.”
elements.passageText5: <sup>9</sup>After he had said this, while they were watching, he was lifted up and a cloud hid him from their sight. <sup>10</sup>As they were staring into the sky while he was going, suddenly two men in white clothing stood near them <sup>11</sup>and said, ‘Men of Galilee, why do you stand here looking up into the sky? This same Jesus who has been taken up from you into heaven will come back in the same way you saw him go into heaven.’

elements.question3: How is the ascension described here? What promise accompanies Jesus’ departure, and what does that mean for the future?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The ascension is described as a visible, witnessed event: the disciples watched as Jesus was lifted up and a cloud took him out of their sight. It was not a quiet disappearance but a deliberate, observable departure. The accompanying promise, delivered by two men in white, is that ’this same Jesus’ will come back in the same way he left. The phrase ’this same Jesus’ is important — not a different figure, but the very one they knew, who died and rose. The ascension therefore points forward: his departure is not the end but a guarantee of his return.
elements.questionHint3: The messengers use the specific phrase ’this same Jesus’ — why might those three words be particularly important as a description of the one who will return?

elements.text3: Where is Jesus now? What authority does He have?

elements.passageText1: <sup>20</sup>1.20 Zab 110.1 wanda ya zartar ga Almasihu sa'ad da ya tashe shi daga matattu, ya kuma ba shi wurin zama a damansa a samaniya, <sup>21</sup>a can a birbishin dukkan sarauta da iko, da ƙarfi, da mulki, a birbishin kowane suna da za a sa, ba ma a duniyar nan kawai ba, har ma a lahira ma. <sup>22</sup>1.22 Zab 8.6 1.22 Kol 1.18 Allah kuma ya sarayar da kome a ƙarƙashin ikon Almasihu, ya kuma ba da shi ga Ikkilisiya ya zama Kai mai mallakar abu duka.
elements.passageText2: <sup>20</sup>wanda ya yi aiki cikin Almasihu saˈad da ya tashe shi daga matattu, ya kuma ba shi wurin zama mai daraja a hannun damansa a sama. 1.20 Zab 110.1 <sup>21</sup>A can Almasihu yake mulki a kan dukan masu sarauta da iko da ƙarfi da mulki. Yana da suna wanda ya fi kowane suna da za a bayar, ba a duniyar nan kaɗai ba, har ma a zamani mai zuwa. <sup>22</sup>Allah kuma ya sa kome a ƙarƙashin ƙafafun Almasihu, ya kuma sa shi ya zama kai mai mulkin abu duka domin amfanin jamaˈar masu bi,. 1.22 Zab 8.7
elements.passageText5: <sup>20</sup>This power he exercised in Christ when he raised him from the dead and seated him at his right hand in the heavenly realms, <sup>21</sup>far above every rule and authority and power and dominion and every name that is named, not only in this age but also in the one to come. <sup>22</sup>And God put all things under Christ’s feet, and he gave him to the church as head over all things,

elements.question3: How does Paul describe Christ’s position and authority? What does it mean that ’all things’ are under his feet?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul describes Christ as seated at God’s right hand — the place of supreme honour and authority — and positioned far above every conceivable power: every rule, authority, dominion, and every name that exists, in this age and the age to come. The image of ’all things under his feet’ is a picture of total sovereignty — nothing lies outside his rule. For believers, this means that the one who is their head and saviour is also the one with ultimate authority over everything that could ever threaten them.
elements.questionHint3: Paul piles up a list of powers that Christ is placed above — what do you think he is trying to convey by using such an exhaustive list rather than just saying ’above everything’?

elements.passageText1: <sup>22</sup>wanda ya tafi Sama, yake dama ga Allah kuma, mala'iku da manyan mala'iku da masu iko suna binsa.
elements.passageText2: <sup>22</sup>Almasihu dai ya tafi sama. Yana a hannun dama na Allah, inda an sa malaˈiku, da masu mulki, da masu iko a ƙarƙashinsa.
elements.passageText5: <sup>22</sup>who went into heaven and is at the right hand of God with angels and authorities and powers subject to him.

elements.question3: What does Peter add to our picture of Christ’s current position? What does it mean for believers that the one who intercedes for them holds this authority?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Peter confirms Christ’s position at God’s right hand, and adds that angels, authorities, and powers are all subject to him — the entire spiritual order is under his command. For believers, this means that the one who prays and pleads for them before the Father is not a petitioner of limited influence; he is the supreme authority in heaven. His intercession on behalf of his people is therefore not uncertain or precarious — it comes from the one to whom everything is already subject.
elements.questionHint3: If the one interceding for you held no authority, what difference might that make? Now consider what it means that he holds all authority — how does that change things?

elements.text3: What is Jesus doing now for His followers?

elements.passageText1: <sup>25</sup>Saboda haka, yana da ikon ceton masu kusatowa ga Allah ta wurinsa, matuƙar ceto, tun da yake kullum a raye yake saboda yi musu addu'a.
elements.passageText2: <sup>25</sup>Saboda haka yana da iko, yau da kullum, yă ceci 7.25 yau da kullum, yă ceci: Wata fassara ta waɗannan kalmomi ita ce “cikakken ceto.” duk waɗanda suke matsowa kusa da Allah ta wurinsa, tun da yake kullum a raye yake, domin yă yi roƙo ga Allah a madadinsu.
elements.passageText5: <sup>25</sup>So he is able to save completely those who come to God through him, because he always lives to intercede for them.

elements.question3: What does the writer say Jesus is always doing? How does the fact that he ’always lives’ to do this shape how you think about your standing before God?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The writer says Jesus always lives to intercede for those who come to God through him — he is perpetually and continuously pleading their cause before the Father. The word ’always’ is significant: this is not an occasional activity but a permanent, unceasing one. For our standing before God, this is deeply reassuring: our security does not depend on our own consistency or performance. Even when we fail, Jesus has not stopped interceding. We are held not by our grip on God but by his grip on us, maintained through Christ’s ongoing work.
elements.questionHint3: The writer says Jesus ’always lives’ to intercede — what would it mean for believers if there were ever a moment when he stopped?

elements.text3: It is easy to think of Jesus’ work as finished — that He died, rose, and then stepped back. But Jesus is not inactive. Right now, He is interceding for His people before the Father. He is our great High Priest, and He never stops.
What can Christians look forward to as a result of the resurrection and ascension of Jesus?

elements.passageText1: <sup>2</sup>A gun Ubana akwai wurin zama da yawa. Da ba haka ne ba, da na faɗa muku, domin zan tafi in shirya muku wuri. <sup>3</sup>In kuwa na je na shirya muku wuri, sai in dawo in kai ku wurina, domin inda nake, ku ma ku zama kuna can.
elements.passageText2: <sup>2</sup>A cikin gidan Ubana akwai wurin zama da yawa. Idan ba haka ba ne da ba zan faɗa muku wannan ba. Za ni can domin in shirya muku wuri. <sup>3</sup>In kuwa na je na shirya muku wuri, zan dawo in kai ku wurina, domin ku ma ku zauna inda nake.
elements.passageText5: <sup>2</sup>There are many dwelling places in my Father’s house. Otherwise, I would have told you, because I am going away to make ready a place for you. <sup>3</sup>And if I go and make ready a place for you, I will come again and take you to be with me, so that where I am you may be too.

elements.question3: What has Jesus gone to prepare, and what does he promise he will do? What does it mean to you that Jesus’ purpose is for his followers to be with him?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus has gone to prepare a place for his followers in his Father’s house, and he promises that he will come again and take them to be with him — so that where he is, they will be too. The goal of the ascension is therefore not absence but reunion. The ultimate destination Jesus describes is not a general ’heaven’ as a place of reward, but being with him specifically. The personal nature of that promise — ‘so that where I am you may be too’ — suggests that the heart of Christian hope is relationship with Christ himself, not merely a pleasant afterlife.
elements.questionHint3: Jesus could have simply promised ’a better place’ — but notice how he describes the destination in personal terms. What does that tell you about what he values most?

elements.text3: Key truths

The resurrection is an essential part of our faith. If Jesus had not been raised from the dead, we would still be condemned by our sins.

‘And if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile; you are still in your sins. … But Christ has indeed been raised from the dead...’ — 1 Corinthians 15:17, 20a

Jesus is now ruling with God the Father in heaven. He acts as a priest, speaking and pleading with the Father on our behalf.

‘...but because Jesus lives for ever, he has a permanent priesthood. Therefore he is able to save completely those who come to God through him, because he always lives to intercede for them.’ — Hebrews 7:24-25
To think about

elements.question3: How would you try to persuade a friend that Jesus did actually rise from the dead?
Which of the Bible verses in this study gives you the greatest comfort or strength? Why?
What, if anything, have you learned from this study which has really surprised you?

elements.text3: Medicine and technology have given us remarkable tools to extend and improve life. But they cannot solve our deepest problem. As we have seen, every one of us has sinned, and we cannot fix that ourselves. So what do we do? In this study we look at how God calls us to respond to everything He has done for us.
Why are we unable to save ourselves from the consequences of our sin?

elements.passageText1: <sup>20</sup>3.20 Zab 143.2; Gal 2.16 Ai, ba wani ɗan adam da zai sami kuɓuta ga Allah ta kiyaye ayyukan Shari'a, tun da yake ta Shari'a ne mutum yake ganin laifinsa.
elements.passageText2: <sup>20</sup>Gama ba wanda zai sami zaman marar laifi a gaban Allah ta hanyar kiyaye abin da Koyarwar ta ce a yi. Abin da Koyarwar take yi shi ne ta nuna wa mutum zunubinsa. 3.20 Zab 143.2; Gal 2.16
elements.passageText5: <sup>20</sup>For no one will be declared righteous in his sight by observing the law; for through the law comes the knowledge of sin.

elements.question3: Why does Paul say that keeping the law cannot make a person right before God? What role does the law actually play, according to this verse?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul says plainly that no one will be declared righteous by observing the law — because the law’s function is not to make us righteous but to reveal that we are not. It acts as a mirror, showing us where we fall short: through the law comes the knowledge of sin. If the law’s purpose is diagnosis, not cure, then trying to use it as a path to salvation is a category error. It can tell us what is wrong; it cannot fix it.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says the law produces ’knowledge of sin’ — if that is what the law gives us, what does that tell you about whether it can also give us the solution?

elements.passageText1: <sup>1</sup>2.1 Kol 2.13 Ku kuma yā raya ku sa'ad da kuke matattu ta wurin laifofinku da zunubanku, <sup>2</sup>waɗanda dā kuke a ciki, kuna biye wa al'amarin duniyar nan, kuna bin sarkin masu iko a sararin sama, wato iskar nan da take zuga zuciyar kangararru a yanzu.
elements.passageText2: <sup>1</sup>A dā ku matattu ne, saboda laifofinku da zunubanku <sup>2</sup>waɗanda dā kuke ciki saˈad da kuke bin hanyoyin duniyar nan, kuna bin sarkin mulki a sammai, 2.2 sarkin mulki a sammai: A nan, ana nufin Shaiɗan ke nan. wato, ruhun nan da yake zuga zuciyar mutanen da suka ƙi biyayya ga Allah.
elements.passageText5: <sup>1</sup>And you were dead in your trespasses and sins, <sup>2</sup>in which you formerly lived according to this world’s present path, according to the ruler of the kingdom of the air, the spirit now at work in the sons of disobedience.

elements.question3: Paul says we were ’dead’ in sins. In what sense is spiritual deadness an obstacle to saving ourselves? How does this image change the way you think about our natural condition?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Paul’s use of ’dead’ is deliberate and radical. A dead person cannot improve themselves, choose differently, or contribute to their own rescue — they need someone from outside to act on them entirely. Spiritual deadness is therefore a complete obstacle to self-salvation: we lack both the ability and the spiritual life to initiate a response to God from our own resources. The image also challenges the common assumption that people are basically good and capable of reaching God through effort — Paul says our starting point is not weakness but death.
elements.questionHint3: Think about what a physically dead person is capable of doing for themselves — how does that image apply to what Paul is saying about our spiritual condition before God?

elements.text3: If we cannot save ourselves, how can we be saved?

elements.passageText1: <sup>8</sup>Domin ta wurin alheri ne aka cece ku saboda bangaskiya, wannan kuwa ba ƙoƙarin kanku ba ne, baiwa ce ta Allah, <sup>9</sup>ba kuwa saboda da aikin lada ba, kada wani ya yi fariya.
elements.passageText2: <sup>8</sup>Ai, ta alherin Allah ne kuka sami ceto ta wurin bangaskiya. Wannan kuwa ba ƙoƙarin kanku ba ne, kyauta ce ta Allah. <sup>9</sup>Ba kuwa saboda aikin lada ba, domin kada wani yă yi taƙama.
elements.passageText5: <sup>8</sup>For by grace you are saved through faith, and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God; <sup>9</sup>it is not from works, so that no one can boast.

elements.question3: Paul says salvation is ’by grace through faith’ and is ’not from yourselves’. What does that rule out? Why does he add that no one can boast?
elements.sampleAnswer3: It rules out any human contribution to salvation — effort, moral achievement, religious observance, or personal merit. Paul is explicit: it is not from yourselves, it is not from works. The reason no one can boast follows directly from that: boasting requires a personal achievement to point to. If salvation is entirely God’s gift from start to finish, there is nothing on our side to be proud of. This also levels the ground completely — no one is more saved than anyone else because they worked harder or were more deserving.
elements.questionHint3: If someone received a gift and then boasted about it, what would that reveal about how they misunderstood where it came from — and how does that apply to salvation?

elements.question: What do you think of when you hear the word grace?
elements.answer: In everyday use, ‘grace’ can mean elegance or poise. In the Bible, it means something quite different: undeserved favour. Grace is God giving us what we do not deserve and could never earn. When the Bible says we are saved ’by grace’, it means our salvation is entirely God’s gift — not a reward for our efforts, our goodness, or our religious activity. This is important because it rules out any boasting on our part, and it means that no one is ever too far gone to receive it.

elements.text3: According to Jesus, what two things are we called to do?

elements.passageText1: <sup>15</sup>1.15 Mat 3.2 yana cewa, “Lokaci ya yi, Mulkin Allah ya kusato. Ku tuba, ku gaskata da bishara.”
elements.passageText2: <sup>15</sup>Yana cewa, “Lokaci ya cika! Mulkin Allah ya yi kusa! Ku tuba, ku gaskata da labari mai daɗi.” 1.15 Mat 3.2
elements.passageText5: <sup>15</sup>and saying, ‘The time is fulfilled and the kingdom of God is near. Repent and believe the gospel!’

elements.question3: Jesus opens his ministry with two commands. What are they, and why do you think he gives them together?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The two commands are: repent and believe the gospel. Jesus gives them together because they are inseparable responses to the same reality — the arrival of God’s kingdom. Repentance without faith would be turning away from something without turning toward anything. Faith without repentance would be a belief that makes no difference to how one lives. Together they describe a complete turning: away from sin and self-direction, and toward God and his good news in Jesus. They are two sides of a single response, not a sequence of separate steps.
elements.questionHint3: Think about what repenting means and what believing means — if you only did one of the two, what would be incomplete about your response?

elements.text3: Repentance … what does it involve?

elements.passageText1: <sup>30</sup>“Domin haka ni Ubangiji Allah zan hukunta ku, ku mutanen Isra'ila, kowa gwargwadon ayyukansa. Ku tuba, ku bar laifofinku domin kada su zama muku dalilin halaka. <sup>31</sup>Ku rabu da laifofin da kuka yi mini, sa'an nan ku samo wa kanku sabuwar zuciya da sabon hali. Don me za ku mutu, ya ku jama'ar Isra'ila?
elements.passageText2: <sup>30</sup>“Domin haka, ya ku Gidan Israˈila, zan shariˈanta ku, kowa bisa ga hanyoyinsa da alˈamuransa, in ji ni Ubangiji Yahweh. Ku tuba, ku juyo daga yin dukan zunubanku na ganganci, in ba haka ba, laifofinku za su zama muku abin tuntuɓe. <sup>31</sup>Ku kawar da dukan zunubanku na ganganci waɗanda kuka yi mini. Ku samo wa kanku sabuwar zuciya da sabon ruhu! Don me za ku mutu, ya ku Gidan Israˈila? 18.31 Ezek 11.19; 36.26
elements.passageText5: <sup>30</sup>Therefore, I will judge each person according to his ways, O house of Israel, declares the sovereign Lord. Repent and turn from all your wicked deeds; then sin will not be an obstacle that brings you down. <sup>31</sup>Throw away all your sins you have committed and fashion yourselves a new heart and a new spirit. Why should you die, O house of Israel?’

elements.question3: What does God call the people to do in these verses? What does ’a new heart and a new spirit’ suggest repentance involves beyond just outward behaviour?
elements.sampleAnswer3: God calls the people to repent, turn from their wicked deeds, and throw away their sins — active language that involves deliberate change. But the deepest demand is for ’a new heart and a new spirit,‘ which points to something far more radical than adjusting outward conduct. The heart in biblical thought is the seat of the will, desires, and identity. Repentance therefore reaches beyond behaviour into motive and character — it is a transformation of what a person loves and lives for, not merely a change in what they do.
elements.questionHint3: God could have just said ’stop doing wrong things’ — why do you think he goes further and asks for a new heart and a new spirit?

elements.passageText1: <sup>20</sup>26.20 A.M 9.20,28,29 Da fari, na yi wa mutanen Dimashƙu wa'azi, sa'an nan na yi a Urushalima da dukan kewayen ƙasar Yahudiya, sa'an nan kuma na yi wa al'ummai, cewa su tuba su juyo ga Allah, su kuma yi aikin da zai nuna tubarsu.
elements.passageText2: <sup>20</sup>Na fara yi wa mutanen Dimashƙu waˈazi, saˈan nan na yi ga mutanen Urushalima da koˈina a yankin Yahudiya, saˈan nan kuma na yi wa waɗanda ba Yahudawa ba cewa su tuba su juyo ga Allah, su kuma yi abubuwan da za su nuna sun tuba. 26.20 A. M. 9.20,28-29
elements.passageText5: <sup>20</sup>but I declared to those in Damascus first, and then to those in Jerusalem and in all Judea, and to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God, performing deeds consistent with repentance.

elements.question3: Paul says true repentance should produce ’deeds consistent with repentance’. What does that suggest about the relationship between inward change and outward action?
elements.sampleAnswer3: It suggests they are connected but distinct: the inward change comes first and is the root, while the outward deeds are the fruit that grows from it. Repentance is not merely a claim or a feeling — it produces visible, consistent change in how a person lives. At the same time, Paul does not say that the deeds are the repentance itself; they are ’consistent with’ it, which means they flow from a genuine inner turning rather than being the turning itself. Outward change without inward change would be performance; inward change without any outward effect would raise serious questions about whether the change was real.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says deeds that are ’consistent with’ repentance — if the deeds are consistent with something, what does that tell you about which comes first, and what produces the other?

elements.text3: Repentance … why is it so important?

elements.passageText1: <sup>3</sup>Ina gaya muku ba haka ba ne! In kuwa ba ku tuba ba, duk za ku halaka kamarsu. ... <sup>5</sup>Ina gaya muku ba haka ba ne! In kuwa ba ku tuba ba, duk za ku hallaka kamarsu.”
elements.passageText2: <sup>3</sup>Sam! Ina gaya muku, idan ba ku tuba a kan laifofinku ba, dukanku za ku halaka kamarsu. ... <sup>5</sup>Sam! Ina gaya muku, in ba ku tuba ba, za ku halaka kamarsu.”
elements.passageText5: <sup>3</sup>No, I tell you! But unless you repent, you will all perish as well! . . . <sup>5</sup>No, I tell you! But unless you repent, you will all perish as well!’

elements.question3: Jesus repeats this warning twice. What does this repetition suggest about its urgency? How would you describe the stakes he is pointing to?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The exact repetition is a rhetorical device that signals this is not a throwaway comment — it is a deliberate, emphatic warning that Jesus wants to land. The stakes are the starkest possible: perishing. Jesus is not describing a minor consequence or a missed opportunity for improvement; he is describing the outcome of a life that does not turn to God. The urgency is correspondingly high — repentance is not optional, or a matter of personal preference, but the hinge on which one’s ultimate fate turns.
elements.questionHint3: Jesus says the same thing twice in the same passage — what effect does exact repetition usually have on the weight of a statement, and what does perishing imply about the severity of the alternative?

elements.text3: Faith … why is this so important?

elements.passageText1: <sup>6</sup>In ba a game da bangaskiya ba kuwa, ba shi yiwuwa a faranta masa rai. Domin duk wanda zai kusaci Allah, lalle ne ya gaskata cewa, akwai shi, yana kuma sakamako ga masu nemansa.
elements.passageText2: <sup>6</sup>In ba tare da bangaskiya ba, ba ya yiwuwa a faranta wa Allah rai, gama duk mai matsowa kusa da Allah dole ne yă ba da gaskiya cewa akwai Allah kuma yana ba da lada ga duk waɗanda suke nemansa.
elements.passageText5: <sup>6</sup>Now without faith it is impossible to please him, for the one who approaches God must believe that he exists and that he rewards those who seek him.

elements.question3: What does the writer say is impossible without faith? What are the two things a person must believe in order to approach God?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Without faith it is impossible to please God. The two things a person must believe in order to approach him are: first, that he exists; and second, that he rewards those who seek him. The first is the foundation — you cannot meaningfully approach a God you do not believe is there. The second is equally necessary — it implies that God is not indifferent or hostile to those who seek him, but actively responds to them. Both beliefs together make genuine approach to God possible; without either, there is no real basis for seeking him.
elements.questionHint3: The writer gives two beliefs, not one — why might it be insufficient to believe that God exists if you did not also believe the second thing?

elements.passageText1: <sup>39</sup>Nufin wanda ya aiko ni kuwa, shi ne kada in yar da kowa daga cikin dukkan waɗanda ya ba ni, sai dai in tashe su a ranar ƙarshe. <sup>40</sup>Gama nufin Ubana, shi ne duk wanda yake duban Ɗan, yake kuma gaskatawa da shi, yă sami rai madawwani, ni kuma zan tashe shi a ranar ƙarshe.”
elements.passageText2: <sup>39</sup>Kuma nufin wanda ya aiko ni shi ne, kada in ɓatar da ko ɗaya daga cikin dukan waɗanda ya ba ni, amma dai in tashe su a ranar ƙarshe. <sup>40</sup>Domin wannan shi ne nufin Ubana cewa, duk wanda yake duban Ɗan, yake kuma gaskatawa da shi yana da rai na har abada, ni kuma zan tashe shi a ranar ƙarshe.”
elements.passageText5: <sup>39</sup>Now this is the will of the one who sent me—that I should not lose one person of every one he has given me, but raise them all up at the last day. <sup>40</sup>For this is the will of my Father—for everyone who looks on the Son and believes in him to have eternal life, and I will raise him up at the last day.’

elements.question3: What does Jesus promise to everyone who looks to him and believes? What does it mean to you that this is described as the Father’s will?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus promises eternal life to everyone who looks to him and believes, and he adds that he will raise them up at the last day — their future is secure even beyond death. The fact that this is described as the Father’s will is significant: it means this is not a plan that could be overruled, revised, or fall through. The saving of those who believe is what God intends and has purposed. It also means that when someone comes to faith, they are not gatecrashing an arrangement — they are stepping into exactly what the Father has willed for them.
elements.questionHint3: Jesus says this is ’the will of my Father’ — if something is God’s will, what does that suggest about how certain or uncertain the outcome is?

elements.passageText1: <sup>27</sup>Tumakin nan nawa sukan saurari muryata, na san su, suna kuma bi na. <sup>28</sup>Ina ba su rai madawwami, ba kuwa za su halaka ba har abada, ba kuma mai ƙwace su daga hannuna.
elements.passageText2: <sup>27</sup>Gama tumakina sukan yi biyayya da abin da nake faɗa, na san su, suna bina kuma. <sup>28</sup>Ina ba su rai na har abada, kuma ba za su taɓa mutuwa ba, kuma ba wanda zai ƙwace su daga hannuna.
elements.passageText5: <sup>27</sup>My sheep listen to my voice, and I know them, and they follow me. <sup>28</sup>I give them eternal life, and they will never perish; no one will snatch them from my hand.

elements.question3: What three things does Jesus say characterise his sheep? What security does he promise them, and how secure does that sound to you?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Jesus says his sheep listen to his voice, he knows them, and they follow him — a mutual relationship of recognition and direction on both sides. The security he promises is absolute: he gives them eternal life, they will never perish, and no one can snatch them from his hand. The language is as strong as it could be: not ’they are unlikely to be lost’ or ’they will be safe if they try hard enough,‘ but they will never perish and no one can remove them. The security rests not on the grip of the sheep but on the grip of the shepherd.
elements.questionHint3: Jesus says no one will ’snatch’ his sheep from his hand — whose hand are they in, and what does that tell you about where the security ultimately comes from?

elements.passageText1: <sup>24</sup>Amma shi da yake madawwami ne, yana da matsayinsa na firist wanda ba ya komawa hannun wani. <sup>25</sup>Saboda haka, yana da ikon ceton masu kusatowa ga Allah ta wurinsa, matuƙar ceto, tun da yake kullum a raye yake saboda yi musu addu'a.
elements.passageText2: <sup>24</sup>Amma Yesu yana riƙe da matsayin firist na har abada, domin shi da kansa yana nan har abada. <sup>25</sup>Saboda haka yana da iko, yau da kullum, yă ceci 7.25 yau da kullum, yă ceci: Wata fassara ta waɗannan kalmomi ita ce “cikakken ceto.” duk waɗanda suke matsowa kusa da Allah ta wurinsa, tun da yake kullum a raye yake, domin yă yi roƙo ga Allah a madadinsu.
elements.passageText5: <sup>24</sup>but he holds his priesthood permanently since he lives forever. <sup>25</sup>So he is able to save completely those who come to God through him, because he always lives to intercede for them.

elements.question3: The writer says Jesus is able to save ’completely’. What grounds that claim? How does his permanent priesthood relate to the security of those who trust him?
elements.sampleAnswer3: The claim is grounded in two related facts: Jesus holds his priesthood permanently because he lives forever, and he always lives to intercede for his people. Unlike any human priest, whose death ends their ministry, Jesus’ priestly work never ceases. ’Completely’ therefore means total and ongoing — not just forgiveness of past sins but continuous intercession that covers every situation. The permanence of his priesthood is the foundation of the security of those who trust him: they are not relying on a representative who might stop, fail, or be replaced.
elements.questionHint3: The writer connects ’save completely’ to the fact that Jesus ’always lives’ — what would change about the completeness of the salvation if Jesus’ intercession could ever stop?

elements.text3: Repentance and faith belong together — you cannot really have one without the other. To repent is to turn away from sin and from living life our own way. To have faith is to turn toward Jesus and trust Him completely. These are not two separate steps; they are two sides of the same turning.
When we choose to follow Jesus, why must we change the way we live?

elements.passageText1: <sup>15</sup>To, ƙaƙa? Wato, sai mu yi zunubi don shari'a ba ta iko da mu, sai alherin Allah? A'a, ko kusa! ... <sup>18</sup>Da aka 'yanta ku kuma daga bautar zunubi, kun zama bayin aikin adalci.
elements.passageText2: <sup>15</sup>Yaya ke nan? Wato, sai mu yi zunubi saboda Koyarwar ba ta da iko a kanmu, sai dai alherin Allah? Ina! Ko kaɗan! ... <sup>18</sup>An ˈyantar da ku daga zunubi, kun kuma zama bayi ga adalci.
elements.passageText5: <sup>15</sup>What then? Shall we sin because we are not under law but under grace? Absolutely not! . . . <sup>18</sup>and since you have been freed from sin, you have become slaves of righteousness.

elements.question3: Paul dismisses the idea that grace is a licence to keep on sinning. What has happened to those who have been saved, and what are they now ’slaves’ to? What does that mean practically?
elements.sampleAnswer3: Those who have been saved have been freed from sin — it no longer has the defining claim over their lives that it once did. But freedom from one master means belonging to another: they have become slaves of righteousness. Paul uses the slavery image deliberately to convey total allegiance and practical daily obedience, not merely a change of status. Practically, it means that the Christian life is not a continuation of the old life with sins forgiven; it is a life actively oriented toward what is right. Grace is not permission to live however one wants — it is the power that makes a new direction of life both possible and required.
elements.questionHint3: Paul says believers have been ’freed from sin’ but immediately adds that they are now ’slaves of righteousness’ — if they have been freed from one thing, why does that freedom mean being bound to something else?

elements.text3: Key truths

Salvation is a free gift from God. There is nothing we can do to earn it or deserve it.

‘For it is by grace you have been saved, through faith – and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God – not by works, so that no one can boast.’ — Ephesians 2:8, 9

There can be no forgiveness without repentance.

‘Peter replied, ‘Repent and be baptised, every one of you, in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins. And you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit...’’ — Acts 2:38
To think about

elements.question3: How would you try to answer your friends who insist that God will accept them because they are essentially good people?
We have been saved by grace. How would you explain the meaning of grace?

elements.text3: It is possible to study the Bible and know it well and, yet, miss the point of why it was written — to show us how we can have a right relationship with God.
The following explanation of how we can know God personally, by believing on the Lord Jesus Christ, may be helpful to those who have yet to make that discovery for themselves.
If you are not already a Christian (or child of God) — or even uncertain what that really means — do read these notes before moving on to Part Two of Truth Unlocked.
Why are we not already in a right relationship with God?

When God created the world, He made us in His likeness so that we might relate with Him and live to glorify Him. But sin spoiled the relationship which God intended, when Adam and Eve disobeyed Him in the Garden of Eden.
We have all inherited that sinful nature.
God is absolutely holy and pure. He cannot tolerate sin and that has become a barrier between God and man.
The Bible tells us: “…for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God,” — Romans 3:23
The Bible tells us: “For the wages of sin is death,…” — Romans 6:23a
If God hates sin, does that mean He is not a God of love?
As God is just, He cannot simply ignore our sin. There has to be a punishment for it.

However, He is certainly loving and merciful. His love is so great that He longs to restore us rather than punish us. He made that possible by sending His Son, Jesus, to come to live on earth as a human being and to die in our place.
The Bible tells us: “But God demonstrates his own love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.” — Romans 5:8
Does that mean God will not punish us?

It means that we will not have to face God’s punishment — but only if we choose to receive God’s forgiveness. We can ask God to forgive us if we believe that Jesus Christ paid the penalty for our sins.
Receiving the forgiveness of our sins is described in the Bible as being ‘born again’. It is the start of a new life as a follower of Jesus.
What happens if we decide not to accept Jesus?

If we say ‘No’ to God’s rescue plan, we will be punished ourselves. That will happen when Jesus returns to earth, for He will come to judge — and punish — everyone who has not accepted God’s offer of forgiveness.
It will be the most awful punishment of being separated from God forever.
The Bible tells us: “…but whoever does not believe stands condemned already because they have not believed in the name of God’s one and only Son.” — John 3:18
What do I need to do to become a Christian?
You can become a follower of Jesus and have all your sins forgiven by:

• accepting that you have not kept God’s laws and deserve to be punished,
  • believing that only Jesus can rescue you from God’s punishment,
  • confessing the wrong things in your life and repenting of them — that is, turning away from them,
  • deciding to live to please God as a follower of Jesus.
If you are ready to take those steps, you can do so now by using the following prayer.

Dear Father God,

I am very sorry for everything in my life, past and present, which is not right.

I now repent of my sin.

Thank You for sending Jesus to die in my place, for my sins, in order to save me from the punishment which I deserve.

I want to live to please Jesus and to make Him the centre of my life — to be my King.

Please forgive all my sins and accept me as Your child. Please fill me with Your Holy Spirit to help me live a new life.

Amen
If you have been sincere with this prayer, God has received you as His child. All your sins — past, present and future — are forgiven.

And what now? You will need to grow as a Christian and that can only happen if you meet regularly with other Christians where the Bible is taught carefully. Therefore, the first step is to link with a local church and with Christians who will be able to help and support you.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Truth Unlocked
studyOnboardingSlides.body:
An eight-session Bible study on the basics of Christian belief.

Work through each chapter at your own pace, or use it with a group.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Read, reflect, write
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Each chapter has Bible questions tied to specific passages, and personal reflection questions at the end. Tap ⓘ beside any Bible reference to read the passage without leaving the app.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Saved on your device
studyOnboardingSlides.body: Your answers are saved automatically as you type, and stored on this device. Use the Share button at the bottom of each chapter to send your answers to a group leader or mentor.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: Adjust to your preference
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you need bigger text go straight to settings now
studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: Tap the ⚙ icon in the top corner to open Settings any time to change text size or switch to dark mode. Tap ☰ (contents) to jump to any chapter.

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: How to use this app
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you want a full guide to getting the most from this app and this study – whether on your own or in a group – you can tap the button below.

action.label: Go to the ‘How to use’ page →

studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: You can also find it any time via the ? icon in the top navigation bar.

blocks.title: People new to Christianity
blocks.body: If you have little or no church background, this study is a good place to start. It doesn’t assume any prior knowledge. It works through the foundations of Christian belief — who God is, who Jesus is, what the Bible says about sin and salvation — and explains each one from the Bible itself. Take it at your own pace, and don’t be afraid to write down the questions that occur to you as you go.

blocks.title: People who grew up in Christian circles
blocks.body: If you have been around church for most of your life, you may find that you have never stopped to ask a basic question: do I actually understand and believe what I think I believe? You know the language, you know the stories, you may even know the Bible well. This study is designed to help you examine the foundations carefully and honestly — not to unsettle you, but to give you something solid to stand on.

blocks.title: People discipling others
blocks.body: If you are walking alongside someone else in their faith — as a mentor, a small group leader, or a friend — this study gives you a structured way to work through the foundations of Christian belief together. The eight studies cover the ground that matters most: who God is, the person and work of Christ, the Holy Spirit, creation, sin, the cross, the resurrection, and our response. Each chapter ends with personal reflection questions that naturally prompt deeper conversation.

blocks.title: On your own
blocks.body: This study works well as a personal study. Work through each chapter at your own pace, read the Bible passages, write your answers to the questions, and meditate on the reflection questions at the end. There is no right speed — some chapters may take twenty minutes, others may take longer if a question stops you in your tracks.

blocks.title: In a group
blocks.body: The study also works well in a small group setting. You could work through each chapter together from scratch, reading the passages and discussing the questions as a group. Alternatively — and this is often the most rewarding approach — each person works through the chapter individually first, writes their answers, and then the group comes together to discuss what they found. Either way works. The questions are designed to prompt genuine reflection, not just right answers.

blocks.title: Working with a leader or mentor
blocks.body:
It can make a real difference to have someone guiding you through the study — a group leader, a pastor, or a more experienced Christian who can help you think through your answers and questions. Perhaps they are the one who recommended this study to you in the first place.

At the end of each chapter, the Share button lets you send your written answers directly to your leader or mentor — via WhatsApp, email, or any other app on your phone. This gives them a window into your thinking before you meet, and makes for much richer conversation.

blocks.title: Two kinds of questions
blocks.body: Each chapter has two kinds of questions. The Bible questions are tied to specific passages — asking you to look carefully at what the text says. The reflection questions at the end are more personal — asking what the chapter means for you specifically.

blocks.title: Common Misunderstanding boxes
blocks.body: Throughout the study you will encounter darker-coloured cards headed Common Misunderstanding. These address words — like grace, repentance, or faith — that Christians use all the time but that are often misunderstood. Take a moment to think about what you understand by the word, then tap Find out to see a short clarification.

leadersNotesData.subtitle: Truth Unlocked
intro: These notes are designed to help group leaders and mentors get the most out of each chapter – both for the people they are leading and for themselves. They are not a substitute for working through the material yourself first. The best leaders are those who have worked through the questions personally before they help others doing the same.

Create a safe space for honesty. The questions in this study are designed to prompt real reflection, not just correct answers. Your job is to make the group feel safe enough to say what they actually think, not what they think a Christian is supposed to say.
Don’t rush the silences. Some of the best moments in a group study happen after a question lands and nobody speaks for a few seconds. Resist the urge to fill every silence.
Watch for the person who goes quiet. Sometimes the most significant thing happening in the room is with the person who says the least. Check in with them afterwards.

chapters.keyPoints:
This chapter establishes the study’s foundation: that we do not have to guess what God is like, because He has revealed Himself. The key movements are (1) the existence of God as evidenced by creation and Scripture, (2) the character of God — holy, eternal, unchanging, omnipresent, and loving — and (3) the decisive demonstration of His love in sending His Son.

The final section on how we know God loves us (Romans 5:8; 1 John 4:9–10) is particularly important: God’s love is not an abstract sentiment but a concrete act, taken before we deserved it or asked for it.
chapters.pastorals:
Many people come to a study like this carrying an image of God that is distant, demanding, or simply vague. The opening question — ‘What is God really like?’ — is worth spending time on. Don’t rush past it to get to the Bible passages.

The passage from Psalm 139:7–10 (God’s omnipresence) can be unsettling for some and comforting for others. Both reactions are worth exploring. The question asks the group to name which it is for them — take that seriously.
chapters.watch: 👁️ Watch for people who intellectually accept God’s existence but have never personally connected that with God’s love for them. The move from ‘God exists’ to ‘God loves me’ is not automatic, and this chapter tries to make it.

chapters.keyPoints:
This chapter makes two essential and complementary claims about Jesus: that He is truly human and truly God. Both matter. His humanity means He genuinely understands our experience. His deity means His death has infinite weight and His words carry ultimate authority.

The Common Misunderstanding callout on ‘Son of God’ addresses a very widespread confusion: that the title implies Jesus is somehow less than God. Ensure the group understands that in its Biblical context it means the opposite.

Hebrews 4:15 — that Jesus was tempted in every way, yet without sin — is both a proof of His humanity and a source of comfort. It should not be rushed past.
chapters.pastorals:
Some in the group may have friends or family of other faiths who honour Jesus as a prophet or teacher but reject His divinity. The material in this chapter gives them something to think through carefully, not as ammunition for arguments, but as an invitation to look at the evidence more closely.

The miracle passages (calming the storm, raising Lazarus) can prompt questions about whether miracles are credible. Acknowledge the question honestly: the chapter is not trying to prove miracles philosophically, but to show what the eyewitnesses recorded and what that implies about who Jesus is.
chapters.watch: 👁️ Watch for people who are comfortable with Jesus as a moral example or spiritual teacher, but resist the claim that He is God. This chapter is designed to press that question gently but clearly.

chapters.keyPoints:
The Holy Spirit is often the least understood person of the Trinity. This chapter establishes three things: (1) the Spirit is a person, not a force or feeling; (2) He is given to every believer, not just some; and (3) His ultimate purpose is to glorify Christ (John 16:14).

The distinction between gifts (1 Corinthians 12) and fruit (Galatians 5) is worth dwelling on. Gifts are distributed sovereignly by the Spirit for the benefit of others; fruit is the character produced over time in every believer by the Spirit’s work.

The Common Misunderstanding callout on ‘the Trinity’ addresses one of the most common sources of confusion in Christian theology. Be prepared for this to open wider questions.
chapters.pastorals:
Different people in the group may come from very different backgrounds when it comes to the Spirit: some may have had charismatic experiences they are unsure about; others may be sceptical of anything experiential. The chapter tries to hold the ground carefully — affirming the Spirit’s genuine and present work, without prescribing what that must look or feel like.

The reflection question about spiritual gifts can be particularly fruitful in a group setting. People often discover gifts they hadn’t named, or are encouraged by others who can see gifts in them that they can’t see in themselves.
chapters.watch: 👁️ Watch for people who equate the Spirit’s work entirely with dramatic visible manifestations, or conversely those who are so cautious that they effectively discount Him altogether. The chapter aims to hold a Biblical centre.

chapters.keyPoints:
This chapter covers the Biblical account of creation: who was involved (all three persons of the Trinity), what was made, how it was made (by God’s word alone), and why — for God’s glory, and for relationship with Him.

The unique dignity of human beings made in the image of God (Genesis 1:27) is central. It distinguishes us from the rest of creation and grounds both our value and our responsibility. The two stewardship passages (Genesis 1:26; 2:15) point to creation care as a genuine theological concern, not a secular add-on.

The Genesis 1 reading callout gives the group a chance to read the full creation account together — worth encouraging even if time is short, as the cumulative effect of the repeated ‘it was good’ and the climactic ‘very good’ carries its own weight.
chapters.pastorals:
Some in the group may be anxious about how to relate the Genesis account to modern science. It is fine to acknowledge that Christians hold a range of views on this, and that the study is not taking a position on the mechanism or timescale of creation — only on the theological claims the text makes. Focus on those.

The reflection question — ‘What does it mean to you to know you are made in God’s image?’ — can be surprisingly moving for people who have rarely been told that their life has inherent worth and dignity.
chapters.watch: 👁️ Watch for the assumption that creation’s goodness is a naive claim in the face of suffering and brokenness. The chapter doesn’t ignore that — it’s precisely the contrast between what God made and what sin has done to it that the next chapter explores.

chapters.keyPoints:
This chapter is the hinge of the study: moving from the goodness of creation to the reality of what has gone wrong. It covers the origin of sin (Genesis 2–3), its nature (rebellion, not just bad behaviour — Mark 7:14–23), its universality (Romans 3:9–12, 23), and its consequences (Romans 6:23).

The Common Misunderstanding callout on ‘sin’ is one of the most important in the study. The everyday reduction of sin to a list of very bad actions is exactly what prevents people from taking seriously that they themselves are sinners. The Biblical definition — self at the centre rather than God — needs time to land.

The closing bridge paragraph is deliberately honest: the picture is bleak. Don’t soften it. The good news of the following chapters only has force if this chapter has done its work.
chapters.pastorals:
This is often the chapter where people most want to push back. Expect it. The claim that everyone has sinned without exception will feel unfair or extreme to some. Take Romans 3:9–12 seriously and let the text speak — Paul anticipates the objection and addresses it directly.

Romans 7:21–25 — Paul’s description of his own internal conflict — is often personally resonant. Give people space to reflect on whether they recognise it in their own experience.
chapters.watch: 👁️ Watch for people who hear the chapter as an attack on their character rather than a diagnosis of the human condition. The point is not that they are especially bad, but that all of us face the same problem.

chapters.keyPoints:
This is where the good news begins. The chapter establishes that God’s response to sin is not condemnation but rescue — and that rescue came at the highest possible cost. The key theological concept is substitutionary atonement: Jesus taking the penalty for our sin in our place (2 Corinthians 5:21; Galatians 3:13; 1 Peter 2:24).

Matthew 27:46 — the cry of dereliction — deserves careful attention. It is not a cry of despair but a theological event: the Son bearing the Father’s judgement on sin. The bridge paragraph after the atonement verses makes this explicit.

The Common Misunderstanding callout on ‘salvation’ reframes the word as rescue — which is its plain meaning and often much more accessible than the religious register in which it is usually heard.
chapters.pastorals:
The section on those who do not believe (John 3:18; 2 Thessalonians 1:5–10) will likely be the most difficult for the group emotionally. Don’t skip it or rush it. The question — ‘How does this sit with you?’ — is intentionally open-ended. Let people be honest.

Some may find the idea of substitutionary atonement morally troubling (‘How can it be just for one person to take another’s punishment?’). This is worth addressing: the answer lies in the fact that it is God Himself, in the person of His Son, who bears the penalty — not an arbitrary third party.
chapters.watch: 👁️ Watch for people who hear ‘Jesus died for us’ as a familiar phrase they have never really stopped to think about. This chapter is designed to make them stop and think about exactly what that means.

chapters.keyPoints:
The resurrection is not an optional extra to the Christian faith — Paul makes this explicit in 1 Corinthians 15:14–19. This chapter covers the evidence for the resurrection (empty tomb, post-resurrection appearances, over 500 witnesses), its significance (death defeated, future resurrection guaranteed), and its continuing implications (Jesus reigning, interceding, and returning).

The bridge paragraph on Jesus’ present ministry (‘He is our great High Priest, and He never stops’) addresses a common assumption: that Jesus finished his work at the cross and ascension. Hebrews 7:25 corrects this — His intercession is continuous and present.

John 14:2–3 closes the chapter on the forward-looking promise of Christ’s return and the believer’s eternal home with Him. Don’t lose this in the apologetic material earlier in the chapter.
chapters.pastorals:
The resurrection is the point at which many people feel the Christian claim becomes hardest to accept. Acknowledge this honestly. The chapter does not try to prove the resurrection philosophically but to present the historical evidence the New Testament offers and ask what best explains it.

The reflection question — ‘Which verse gives you the greatest comfort or strength?’ — is a good way to close. It moves from the apologetic register of the chapter into something more personal and devotional.
chapters.watch: 👁️ Watch for people who treat the resurrection as a metaphor (Jesus ‘lives on’ in memory or influence). The passages in this chapter — particularly Luke 24:36–43 and 1 Corinthians 15:5–8 — are insisting on a physical, bodily, historical event.

chapters.keyPoints:
This final chapter asks the question the whole study has been building toward: how do we respond? The two responses the Bible calls for are repentance and faith — and the chapter is careful to hold them together as two sides of the same turning, not two separate steps.

The Common Misunderstanding callout on ‘grace’ is crucial here. Salvation is entirely God’s gift (Ephesians 2:8–9) — not a reward for effort, goodness, or religious activity. This must be clear before the call to repentance is heard correctly, or repentance itself becomes a work we perform to earn God’s favour.

The closing section on changed living (Romans 6:15, 18) makes clear that grace is not a licence to continue in sin. Those who have been freed from sin have become servants of righteousness. The Christian life is not a static event but an ongoing reorientation.
chapters.pastorals:
This is the chapter most likely to prompt a personal response. Be alert to people who are ready to make a commitment but don’t know how to do it, or who feel they have too much to give up. Be ready to talk personally with individuals after the group session.

The distinction between repentance as genuine inward change (not just regret or reform) and faith as active trust (not just intellectual assent) is important and worth drawing out carefully. Both can be counterfeited, and both can be misunderstood.
chapters.watch: 👁️ Watch for people who hear the call to repentance and conclude that they must clean up their life before they can come to God. The order in the gospel is the opposite: come as you are, and God will begin the transformation from there.

paras: Rick Creighton grew up in Northern Ireland and studied maths at Oxford and then taught maths at a secondary school in England. After that, he studied theology at Moore Theological College in Sydney and London School of Theology.

He served as a church pastor in England for ten years, before moving to Nigeria in 2012, where he has been teaching at ECWA Theological Seminary Kagoro and Jos ECWA Theological Seminary. He has been involved with BeaconLight Training since 2003.
Rick is married to Alanna. They have three children: Asha, Conor, and Jack.

publisher.name: BeaconLight
paras: BeaconLight is a non-denominational Christian training organisation based in the UK, founded in 1995. Its aim is to equip Christians worldwide with practically-applied Biblical gospel ministry tools — resources designed to help people both equip themselves and equip others.

BeaconLight’s resources include CrossCheck, which enables Christians to explain their faith and lead others to Jesus, and Word@Work, a verse-by-verse applied devotional commentary of the whole New Testament. BeaconLight also provides printed Biblical resources to prisons, seafarers, police, and the armed services.
BeaconLight believes that Christian ministry should be free of charge, and all its online materials are available without registration or payment. The ministry is funded entirely by donations.
BeaconLight is a member of the Evangelical Alliance and Global Connections, and is a registered charity in England and Wales (Charity No. 1047046).
